Using bar models alongside abstract calculations is a useful strategy to support students initially. Encourage students to identify which of the two coefficients of the unknown is the smallest, ensuring students see examples of equations with the greater coefficient of an unknown on the right side as well as the left side. Practice should include both non-integer and negative solutions. Students should also have opportunities to solve equations involving negative coefficients of an unknown, such as 20 – x = 4x + 5 Encourage students to check solutions by using substitution.
SS4_desc: In this small step, students interpret and use inequalities, building on prior learning from Key Stage 3. Begin by reviewing the meaning of each inequality symbol, highlighting key concepts such as the difference between x < 3 and x ≤ 3 Students should also understand inequalities where the unknown is on the right-hand side, appreciating that, for example, m < 8 is equivalent to 8 > m. Encourage discussions around integer solutions that satisfy a given inequality and introduce compound inequalities such as 3 < y ≤ 7 When working with number lines, ensure that students understand the associated notation, such as which inequality symbols correspond to shaded or unshaded circles.
SS5_desc: In this small step, students will recap solving linear inequalities. It is useful to highlight similarities between equations and inequalities, as the strategies to solve both are similar. Students need to understand a linear equation has one solution, whereas a linear inequality has a range of solutions. Make links to the previous step, discussing possible integer solutions and provide opportunities for students to represent these on a number line. Once students are confident, they could solve inequalities with unknowns on both sides. If appropriate, students can also solve compound inequalities such as 4 ≤ 2x < 12 or inequalities with negative coefficients of an unknown, such as –2m < 10
SS6_desc: In this small step, students will learn how to represent the solutions to inequalities using formal set notation. Students will make links between number line representations, verbal descriptions and formal set notation. Highlight that a colon in set notation can be interpreted as “such that”. For example, {x : x < 3} can be read as “the set of values of x is less than 3”. Once students can represent simple solution sets, introduce sets using the “union”, for example {x : x < 2}  {x : x > 5}. Students may be familiar with this from the probability block in Year 9. Representing these on a number line is a useful visual aid to highlight values that do not satisfy these inequalities.
SS7_desc: In this small step, students rearrange simple formulae that involve one or two steps of working, to change the subject. Bar models and function machines can be useful representations to support students’ understanding. Drawing comparisons to solving equations is useful as students begin to explore this concept. Ensure that students understand the subject of a formula is the variable that is isolated and equal to an expression involving other variables. Discuss the fact that the subject of a formula can appear on either the left or right side of the equals sign. For example, r is the subject of both r = m + p and m + p = r.
SS8_desc: In this small step, students rearrange known formulae, building on learning from Key Stage 3. Students use rearrangements of formulae to solve problems, such as making d the subject of C = πd to work out the diameter of a circle when given the circumference. This provides students with a context of why changing the subject of a formula is a useful skill. If appropriate, students could be asked to recall known formulae rather than having formulae provided. Rewriting formulae involving fractions may be useful to highlight key steps when rearranging. For example, writing A = 1 2 bh as A = bh 2 to highlight that both sides of the formula can be multiplied by 2 as a first step to make b or h the subject.
SS9_desc: In this small step, students rearrange complex formulae involving combinations of squares, square roots, fractions and brackets. Explore strategies to rearrange formulae where the proposed subject has a negative coefficient, such as making t the subject of y = x – t. Comparing this to solving a “subtract from” equation such as 5 = 12 – t could be useful. Students could then move on to formulae like this involving fractions. Once students are confident, introduce formulae involving brackets, while comparing different strategies, such as expanding a bracket or dividing by the coefficient of the bracket. Students should also be given opportunities to rearrange formulae with squared terms and/or respective roots.
SS10_desc: In this small step, students change the subject of a formula where the proposed subject appears more than once. For example, making t the subject of at + bt = c. Begin with formulae involving brackets, highlighting that dividing by an expression can be a useful strategy to rearrange formulae in this form. For example, dividing both sides of the formula by (a + b) to make x the subject of x(a + b) = c. Encourage students to isolate like terms involving the proposed subject before factorising and then completing the rearrangement to isolate the subject. If appropriate, students could explore rearranging formulae involving fractions, such as making y the subject of T = x y y +
SS1_desc_short: Students revisit 1- and 2-step equations from Key Stage 3, progressing to complex equations. Tools like bar models highlight inverse operations. They'll solve various equation forms, including those with brackets, non-integer/negative solutions, and negative coefficients. Encourage solution checking via substitution. Calculators can be used.
SS2_desc_short: Students revisit solving fractional equations, using function machines to distinguish between equations like x 5 + 1 = 13 and x +1 5 = 13. They discuss the order of inverse operations and practice various equation forms. As confidence grows, complex equations, including those with non-integer or negative solutions, are introduced.
SS3_desc_short: Students learn to solve equations with unknowns on both sides, identifying the smaller coefficient. Bar models and abstract calculations are used for support. Practice includes non-integer and negative solutions, and equations with negative coefficients. Students check solutions through substitution. Examples feature greater coefficients on both sides.
SS4_desc_short: Students interpret and use inequalities, building on Key Stage 3 knowledge. Review inequality symbols, differences like x < 3 and x ≤ 3, and right-hand side unknowns (m < 8 equals 8 > m). Discuss integer solutions for inequalities and introduce compound inequalities (3 < y ≤ 7). Understand notation in number lines.
SS5_desc_short: Students will review solving linear inequalities, noting similarities with equations. They'll learn that equations have one solution, while inequalities have a range. They'll represent integer solutions on a number line, solve inequalities with unknowns on both sides, and tackle compound inequalities or those with negative coefficients.
SS6_desc_short: Students will learn to represent inequality solutions using set notation, linking number line representations and verbal descriptions. The colon in set notation means "such that". They'll practice with simple sets and "union" sets, and use number lines to visualize values not satisfying inequalities.
SS7_desc_short: Students learn to rearrange simple formulae in one or two steps, changing the subject. Bar models and function machines aid understanding. Comparisons to equation solving are beneficial. Students should understand the subject of a formula is the isolated variable, which can appear on either side of the equals sign.
SS8_desc_short: Students build on Key Stage 3 learning to rearrange formulae, such as making 'd' the subject of C = πd. This teaches why changing the subject of a formula is useful. Students may recall known formulae and rewrite those involving fractions, like A = 1/2bh as A = bh/2.
SS9_desc_short: Students learn to rearrange complex formulae involving squares, square roots, fractions, and brackets. They tackle negative coefficients, like making t the subject of y = x – t, and progress to formulae with fractions. Strategies include expanding brackets or dividing by the bracket's coefficient. Practice with squared terms and roots is also provided.
SS10_desc_short: Students learn to rearrange formulae where the subject appears multiple times, such as at + bt = c. They start with formulae involving brackets, using division to isolate the subject, like x in x(a + b) = c. They isolate like terms, factorise, and complete the rearrangement. Advanced students may tackle formulae with fractions.

Small Step 7: Solve more complex quadratic equations by factorisation (E)
Small Step 9: Complete the square with more complex quadratic expressions (E)
Small Step 10: Solve quadratic equations by completing the square (E)
Small Step 11: Solve quadratic equations using the quadratic formula
SS1_desc: In this small step, students will build on their knowledge of expanding double brackets in the form (ax ± b)(cx ± d) from Key Stage 3. Begin with expressions in the form (x + a)(x + b), with all positive terms, and introduce negative terms as students’ confidence develops, before introducing expressions with coefficients of the variable. Expressions in unfamiliar forms such as (x + 2)2 or (x + 2)(3 + x) can also be explored. Recap the use of an area model as a visual prompt for discussion on how to expand binomials, using algebra tiles to support if necessary. Students need to be confident with simplification and calculating with negative numbers. Where appropriate, extend students by expanding brackets in other mathematical contexts, for example, expressing the area of rectilinear shapes with binomial dimensions.
SS2_desc: In this small step, students explore expanding triple brackets in the form (ax ± b)(cx ± d)(ex ± f). Students may have previously encountered this topic as an extend step in Year 9 Build on their knowledge of expanding double brackets by systematically modelling the expansion and simplification of the first two brackets before expanding the result by the third bracket. Using a multiplication grid can help students organise their work and ensure all terms are included. Encourage students to check that every term is multiplied. As their confidence develops, introduce negative terms and other triple-bracket formats such as (x + 2)3 and (x – 2)2(3 + x).
SS3_desc: In this small step, students will factorise quadratic expressions in the form x2 ± bx ± c. Students may have previously covered this in Key Stage 3. Support students to make connections by explicitly showing that factorising is the inverse of expanding brackets. Begin by asking students to find two numbers that make a specific sum and product, for example, find two numbers that have a sum of 7 and a product of 10 Students should first factorise expressions in the form x2 + bx + c. Once students are confident, progress onto factorising expressions such as x2 + 7x – 30 and x2 – x – 12 Using algebra tiles and multiplication grids can strengthen students’ conceptual understanding.
SS4_desc: In this small step, students will factorise quadratic expressions of the form ax2 ± bx ± c where a > 1. Emphasise that they need to consider both the factors of the x2 coefficient and the factors of the constant term when factorising. Start with expressions where a is a prime number. Using algebra tiles can support understanding, allowing students to determine the correct factor pairs for a and c that will sum to b when expanded. As confidence develops, students can progress to a more abstract method, using trial and improvement to find the correct factor pairs. Draw students’ attention to how the position of the factors within the bracket affects the final answer, for example (3x + 4)(2x + 5) is different to (3x + 5)(2x + 4). Encourage students to expand the brackets afterwards to check their factorisation.
SS5_desc: In this small step, students will explore expressions in the form x2 – a2 where a is an integer. Encourage students to expand brackets of the form (x ± a)(x ± a) to uncover the difference of two squares. Explore the connection to quadratics in the form x2 ± bx ± c, noting how the middle term bx is eliminated. Highlight to students that, for example, in the expression x2 – 36, the value of b is 0, hence why it is omitted. Then encourage students to consider a factor pair of –36 that sum to 0, to enable them to factorise the expression. Repeat with other expressions in this form, encouraging students to spot that the value of c is always a square number. Once students are confident, challenge them to factorise expressions like 4x2 – 25 If appropriate, explore fully factorising expressions such as 48 – 3y2
SS6_desc: In this small step, students will solve quadratic expressions equal to zero in the form (x + a)(x + b) = 0. The purpose of this step is to prepare students for solving quadratic equations by factorisation. Firstly, practise solving linear equations equal to zero such as x + 2 = 0 and ab = 0, varying the value of a or b before solving. Draw students’ attention to the fact that if the product of two numbers or terms is zero, then at least one of the two numbers or terms must be zero. This understanding helps explain why quadratic equations can have up to two solutions. Remind students that (x + a)(x + b) = 0 represents (x + a) × (x + b) = 0, and so to solve it, the value of either bracket must be 0 Once students are confident, progress to solving equations with coefficients of x, for example, (3x + 4)(2x – 1).
SS7_desc: In this small step, students will solve quadratic equations by first factorising the equation. Students may be familiar with solving quadratic equations from Year 9, as this was previously covered as an extend step. Building on students’ understanding from the previous step, begin with factorising quadratic equations that are equal to zero before asking students to solve. Emphasise that quadratic equations must be equal to zero before they can be solved by factorisation. Encourage students to check their solutions by substituting into the original equation. Once students are confident, challenge them to solve quadratic equations that are not equal to zero and require rearranging before solving, for example, x2 + 7x = –12 Make links between solving a quadratic and the graphical representation of the quadratic using dynamic software.
SS8_desc: In this small step, students will solve quadratics by first factorising expressions in the form ax2 ± bx ± c. This will build on students’ understanding of solving quadratic equations from the previous step, but it will now incorporate those where the coefficient of x2 is greater than 1 Encourage students to form equations for each bracket. For example, (3x + 4)(2x – 1) = 0 can be solved using 3x + 4 = 0 and 2x – 1 = 0 before solving these separately to find the solutions of x. Encourage students to check their solutions by substituting into the original expression. Once students are confident, challenge them to solve expressions that require rearranging. For example, 4x2 + 10x + 8 = 6 + 3x – x2
SS9_desc: In this small step, students will complete the square with quadratic expressions in the form x2 ± bx ± c by writing them in the form (x ± p)2 ± q. It is useful to begin by showing students examples of expressions that form a perfect square, for example, how x2 + 6x + 9 can be factorised to (x + 3)2 Remind students that not all quadratic expressions can be factorised this way, but completing the square allows any quadratic expression to be rewritten as a single bracket squared, +/– a constant. Use algebra tiles to demonstrate the “square” and visualise why the coefficient of x is halved to find p and how to determine the value of q. Begin with expressions that have all positive terms and an even coefficient of x before introducing negative coefficients and odd coefficients of x. Discuss with students how completing the square can later be applied to solve quadratic equations and to find the turning point of a quadratic graph. These topics will be covered later this year.
SS10_desc: In this small step, students will complete the square with quadratic expressions in the form ax2 ± bx ± c where a > 1 and rewrite them in the form a(x ± p)2 ± q. Remind students how to complete the square for quadratic expressions where the coefficient of x2 is 1 Direct students to factor out the coefficient of x2, to bring a sense of familiarity from the previous step of completing the square, before expanding by the factorised coefficient of x2 Highlight to students the use of square brackets here to separate the different factorisations. As students’ confidence grows, introduce expressions where all terms are not a factor of the coefficient of x2 and the use of fractional terms is necessary, such as 2x2 + 4x – 3 ≡ 2 x2 + 2x – 3 2
SS11_desc: In this small step, students will solve quadratic equations by completing the square. This step builds on students’ understanding of solving equations from the previous block and the process of completing the square. Ensure students are confident solving equations involving squared terms, negative values, and fractions. Begin with expressions in the form x2 + bx + c = 0, where b is a positive even number, to reinforce the method. Then, as students develop confidence, introduce negative and odd coefficients of x. Remind students that when trying to solve equations such as x2 = 16, there are both positive and negative roots, which is why quadratic equations often have two solutions. Model leaving solutions in exact form as well as rounded answers from a calculator. Encourage students to consider when it may be necessary to complete the square instead of factorising when solving quadratics.
SS1_desc_short: Students will expand on Key Stage 3 knowledge of double brackets, starting with positive terms and progressing to negative. They'll explore unfamiliar expressions, use area models and algebra tiles for binomial expansion, and simplify calculations with negative numbers. Advanced students may expand brackets in other contexts, like rectilinear shapes' binomial dimensions.
SS2_desc_short: Students expand triple brackets like (ax ± b)(cx ± d)(ex ± f), building on prior knowledge of double brackets. They model expansion and simplification of two brackets before adding the third. A multiplication grid aids organization and ensures all terms are multiplied. As confidence grows, negative terms and formats like (x + 2)3 and (x – 2)2(3 + x) are introduced.
SS3_desc_short: Students will factorise quadratic expressions like x2 ± bx ± c, building on Key Stage 3 knowledge. They'll learn factorising as the inverse of expanding brackets, starting with expressions like x2 + bx + c. Progress includes factorising x2 + 7x – 30 and x2 – x – 12. Algebra tiles and multiplication grids aid understanding.
SS4_desc_short: Students will factorise quadratic expressions (ax2 ± bx ± c, a > 1), considering factors of both x2 coefficient and constant term. Start with prime 'a' values. Use algebra tiles for understanding and trial and improvement for abstract methods. Note how factor positions affect the answer and check by expanding brackets.
SS5_desc_short: Students will examine expressions like x2 – a2, where a is an integer. They'll expand brackets (x ± a)(x ± a) to discover the difference of two squares, and relate this to quadratics x2 ± bx ± c. They'll learn to factorise expressions by identifying factor pairs and recognising that c is always a square number.
SS6_desc_short: Students will solve quadratic expressions (x + a)(x + b) = 0, preparing for quadratic equations' factorisation. They'll practice solving linear equations like x + 2 = 0, understanding if a product equals zero, one term must be zero. This explains why quadratic equations have two solutions. Progress to equations with x coefficients.
SS7_desc_short: Students will solve quadratic equations by factorising, building on prior knowledge from Year 9. They'll start with equations equal to zero, then progress to those requiring rearrangement. Emphasis is on checking solutions by substituting into the original equation and linking solutions to graphical representations using dynamic software.
SS8_desc_short: Students will solve complex quadratic equations by factorising expressions like ax2 ± bx ± c, building on prior knowledge. They'll form separate equations from brackets, solve them, and verify solutions by substituting into the original expression. Once confident, they'll tackle expressions needing rearrangement.
SS9_desc_short: Students learn to complete the square with quadratic expressions (x2 ± bx ± c), rewriting them as (x ± p)2 ± q. Examples and algebra tiles illustrate perfect squares and coefficient halving. This method applies to all quadratics, including those with negative/odd coefficients, and aids in solving equations and finding graph turning points.
SS10_desc_short: Students will learn to complete the square with quadratic expressions (ax2 ± bx ± c, a > 1), rewriting them as a(x ± p)2 ± q. They'll factor out the coefficient of x2, use square brackets for factorisations, and handle expressions with non-factor terms and fractional terms.
SS11_desc_short: Students will solve quadratic equations using the 'completing the square' method, building on prior knowledge of equation-solving. They'll start with expressions like x2 + bx + c = 0, then tackle negative and odd coefficients. They'll learn about positive and negative roots, exact solutions, and when to complete the square.

Small Step 4: Express one number as a fraction or percentage of another
Small Step 5: Express a change as a percentage
Small Step 6: Find the original value after a percentage change
Small Step 9: Choose appropriate methods to solve percentage problems
SS1_desc: In this small step, students find percentages of an amount using both calculator and non-calculator methods. Students need to be confident with both and would benefit from having a bank of different methods for each to support them answering a variety of different question types. A good discussion point is around which method is most appropriate and efficient in each scenario, although this can be discussed retrospectively if students would benefit more from just completing the calculations initially. Start by finding different percentages of simple numbers to focus on methods before increasing arithmetic complexity by using more challenging percentages and numbers. The use of multipliers supports efficiency by using a single calculation and supports students with later steps in the block. This step also provides an opportunity to incorporate contextual links, such as working out the amount of tax a person pays.
SS2_desc: Building on from the previous small step, students use calculator and non-calculator methods to increase and decrease by a given percentage. When answering these questions, a useful starting question is to ask students, “Will the answer be greater than or less than the original amount?”, linking to their existing understanding of the words “increase” and “decrease”. This will support them in choosing the appropriate steps to complete their calculations, as well as being able to sense check their answers. Where the question doesn’t explicitly state the word “increase” or “decrease”, prompt students to interpret the context and recognise what is being asked.
SS3_desc: In this small step, students explore repeated percentage change involving both percentage increases and decreases. This will support students when later exploring compound interest, which is a specific example of repeated percentage change. Examples should include those where the percentage an amount is increasing or decreasing by remains constant, and also examples where it changes. Students should also explore growth and decay problems. Multipliers are the most efficient method when the repeated change is with the same percentage. Encourage discussions around why the sum of individual percentage changes does not equate to their combined effect over time. For example, a 5% increase followed by a 10% increase is not equivalent to a 15% increase of the original value.
SS4_desc: In this small step, students express one number as a fraction or a percentage of another using both calculator and non-calculator methods. It is useful to start with fractions, as students’ common terminology around fractions will support their understanding. For example, 11 50 is often read as “eleven fiftieths” or “11 out of 50”, and the latter supports this learning. Once confident in this, students can then use their understanding of percentages being out of 100 and equivalent fractions to express one number as a percentage of another, before moving on to more complex examples where they express any number as a fraction or percentage of another. Encourage students to check their answers by calculating the fraction or percentage of the amount to ensure it is accurate.
SS5_desc: Building on from the previous small step, students now write a change between two amounts as a percentage. Encourage students to identify and label the original and new amounts from the question so that they can correctly identify whether the change is an increase or decrease. From this, they then calculate the difference in the amounts and use the skills from the previous step to express this difference as a percentage of the original amount. Students become familiar with the general formula change original × 100 to express the percentage change. It is useful to apply contexts in this step such as changes in population figures. This skill also commonly arises in science questions. For example, when investigating osmosis, students calculate the percentage change in mass of a potato.
SS6_desc: Building on the previous small step, students now find the original value given a percentage or a percentage change. Ensure that students understand that the original amount is represented by 100%. Using and comparing common everyday life contexts can support students in understanding the difference between this step and the earlier step on calculating a percentage of an amount. Prompt students to consider whether the given amount represents 100% and explain why. Bar models can also be a useful tool to support students in visualising information and highlighting the percentage that the amount represents. They can be particularly useful when students are faced with a mixture of questions and deciding which skill is being tested.
SS7_desc: In this small step, students explore how to calculate simple interest. They need to understand that simple interest means that the amount of interest added does not change, and they do not need to recalculate the interest at the end of each time period. This step is a great opportunity to include elements of financial literacy to prepare them for life beyond education. Whilst not the most common type of interest in real life, simple interest can be applied in products such as personal loans, short-term investments and bonds. Whilst compound interest isn’t covered until the next step, it can be useful to discuss the benefits of simple interest with students by asking questions such as, “Is it positive or negative that the interest rate is always calculated on the original amount? Why?”.
SS8_desc: In this small step, students explore compound interest, understanding how it differs from simple interest. Students understand that, unlike simple interest, the amount of interest changes each time period based on the new value. This small step is an application of repeated percentage change. To further support financial literacy, consider spending time comparing and contrasting simple and compound interest and the practical implications of each. When teaching compound interest, it can be useful to work through and set out each time period individually to clearly highlight the differences between compound and simple interest, before moving on to a multiplier method for efficiency.
SS9_desc: This small step allows students the opportunity to practise a diverse range of percentage problems where their choice of method is not prescriptive but may impact on efficiency and accuracy. Both calculator and non-calculator problems should be explored. Ensure there are questions where the original amount is given, or to be found from a percentage, to tackle students’ overgeneralisations such as always finding the percentage of the given amount first. Bar models may support students in choosing the correct calculation to solve the problem. If appropriate, students could explore using properties of commutativity to solve percentage problems, for example, 64% of 25 = 25% of 64 Encourage students to check whether their answer is reasonable by considering it in the context of the question.
SS1_desc_short: Students learn to calculate percentages of amounts using calculator and non-calculator methods. They explore various methods, discussing efficiency and appropriateness for different scenarios. The focus starts on simple numbers, then progresses to complex arithmetic. Multipliers enhance efficiency and prepare students for future steps. Contextual links, like tax calculations, are incorporated.
SS2_desc_short: Building on prior knowledge, students use calculator and non-calculator methods to adjust amounts by a given percentage. They learn to predict if results will be greater or less than the original, choose correct calculation steps, and validate their answers. They also interpret questions without explicit "increase" or "decrease" cues.
SS3_desc_short: Students examine repeated percentage change, including increases, decreases, and constant or varying rates. This aids in understanding compound interest and growth/decay problems. Using multipliers is efficient for consistent percentages. Discuss why sequential percentage changes don't equal their total over time, like differing results from consecutive 5% and 10% increases.
SS4_desc_short: Students learn to express one number as a fraction or percentage of another, using calculator and non-calculator methods. Starting with fractions aids understanding. They then apply knowledge of percentages and equivalent fractions to more complex examples. Checking answers ensures accuracy.
SS5_desc_short: Building on prior steps, students write percentage changes between two amounts, identifying original and new amounts to determine increase or decrease. They calculate the difference, express it as a percentage of the original, and apply the formula change original × 100. This skill is useful in contexts like population changes or scientific investigations.
SS6_desc_short: Students now identify the original value from a given percentage or percentage change, understanding that 100% represents the original amount. This step differs from earlier percentage calculations. Students should consider if the given amount equals 100%. Bar models aid in visualising information and identifying the tested skill.
SS7_desc_short: Students learn to calculate simple interest, understanding it remains constant over time. This step introduces financial literacy concepts, applying simple interest to real-life scenarios like personal loans and short-term investments. It encourages discussions on the benefits of simple interest, questioning its impact on the original amount.
SS8_desc_short: Students learn about compound interest, distinguishing it from simple interest by observing how interest changes each period based on the new value. This step applies repeated percentage change. Teaching includes comparing simple and compound interest, detailing each time period, and introducing a multiplier method for efficiency.
SS9_desc_short: Students practice diverse percentage problems, using various methods impacting efficiency and accuracy. Both calculator and non-calculator problems are explored. Questions include finding original amounts from percentages to challenge overgeneralisations. Bar models aid in problem-solving. Commutativity properties may be used. Students check answer reasonability within the question's context.

macro_topic: Ratio, proportion and rates of change
Small Step 2: Share in a ratio (whole, part or difference given)
Small Step 6: Solve problems with ratio and algebra
SS1_desc: In this small step, students find equivalent ratios and simplify ratios, building on their learning from Key Stage 3. Remind students to identify the highest common factor of the numbers in a given ratio and highlight that dividing by the highest common factor will give a ratio in its simplest form. Other strategies can also be explored, such as dividing the ratio 24 : 18 by 2, and then by 3. Ensure that students understand that equivalent ratios are multiples or factors of each other and discuss how to work out equivalent ratios, for example by doubling both numbers in a given ratio. Discuss with students how ratios involving units such as money, time or length need to be re-written using the same units before they can be simplified. If appropriate, students could explore writing ratios in the form 1 : n or n : 1. Note that this is an extend step in Year 8, so some students may not have covered this before.
SS2_desc: In this small step, students solve ratio problems given the whole, part or difference, building on learning from Key Stage 3. Comparison bar models are recommended for this step as they support students in understanding the calculations required. Ensure students’ attention is drawn to where given information is labelled. Discuss the importance of drawing relatively accurate bar models as those that are not proportional can lead to incorrect calculations. Comparing problems where a part, whole or difference is given will be useful to develop a deeper understanding of ratio problems in different contexts. Provide opportunities to explore problems in a range of contexts as well as ratios that compare three or more quantities.
SS3_desc: In this small step, students explore how the parts in a ratio compare to each value in the ratio as a fraction of the whole, building on learning from Year 8. Single bar models can be useful for highlighting the differences between fractions and ratios. Remind students that a ratio compares one part of a whole to another part, whereas a fraction compares one part to the whole. Provide students with the opportunity to write a ratio as a fraction and a fraction as a ratio. Students should also be given opportunities to use fractions to compare ratios, such as the ratio of water to juice in two different drinks to decide which is stronger. Students may need to revisit strategies for comparing fractions, such as writing equivalent fractions and comparing the numerators when the denominators are the same.
SS4_desc: In this small step, students use their knowledge of equivalent ratios to combine two ratios. Remind students how to identify the lowest common multiple of two numbers and ensure that they can work with equivalent ratios. Support students by modelling the use of language such as “for every red, there are yellow”. In addition, encourage students to identify common parts between ratios. For example, the ratios red : blue and blue : yellow both include “blue”. Highlight that the common parts in ratios must be equal to combine them and explore methods for this, such as scaling one of the given ratios. Extra challenge can be added by changing the common term. For example, providing the ratios red : blue and red : yellow.
SS5_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding of ratios and apply them to an algebraic context. Students must recognise that in a given ratio a : b, a + b represents the total. As with previous steps, bar models are a useful representation that can help students make links between worded ratio problems and those presented algebraically. Students can also explore algebraic ratio problems involving a difference. For example, working out the values of x and y when the ratio of x to y is 2 : 3 and y – x = 8. If appropriate, explore algebraic problems that involve skills learned earlier in the block, such as combining a pair of ratios represented algebraically or writing algebraic ratios as fractions.
SS6_desc: In this small step, students form and solve equations using equivalent ratios. Note that some students may not have covered this before, as it is an extend step in Year 9. Introduce algebraic ratios equivalent to 1 : 1 first, before moving on to other unitary ratios such as 2x : 20 = 1 : 2, highlighting that 2x ≠ 20. Discuss the relationships between the parts in the ratio. For example, “the second part of the ratio is double the first part”. Then, model how to manipulate expressions to make an equation, such as multiplying 2x by 2 to form the equation 4x = 20 Bar models can help support students in forming equations. If appropriate, students could also explore algebraic ratios equivalent to non-unitary ratios, such as x : x + 5 = 2 : 3, as well as forming and solving quadratic equations.
SS7_desc: In this small step, students make links between scales on a map or diagram and ratios. Ensure that students are confident with metric conversions such as converting centimetres to metres. Emphasise how scales must involve the same units when represented in a ratio. For example, if 1 cm represents 3 m, this cannot be represented using the ratio 1 : 3 because two different units are used. Provide students with strategies to convert centimetres to kilometres, such as converting to metres first, before converting to kilometres. Students should be encouraged to interpret scale diagrams, making links between the distances on the map/plan and the actual distances they represent.
SS1_desc_short: Students build on Key Stage 3 learning to find and simplify equivalent ratios using strategies like identifying the highest common factor. They learn that equivalent ratios are multiples or factors of each other and must be rewritten in the same units (money, time, length) for simplification.
SS2_desc_short: Students solve ratio problems using comparison bar models, building on Key Stage 3 knowledge. Emphasize accurate model drawing and label understanding to avoid calculation errors. Discuss ratio problems with varying given parts for deeper comprehension. Explore problems in diverse contexts and ratios comparing multiple quantities.
SS3_desc_short: Students learn to compare ratio parts as fractions of the whole, using single bar models to distinguish between fractions and ratios. They practice writing ratios as fractions and vice versa, and use fractions to compare ratios, like water to juice ratios in drinks. Strategies for comparing fractions are revisited.
SS4_desc_short: Students apply knowledge of equivalent ratios and lowest common multiples to combine two ratios. They identify common elements in ratios, ensuring equality for combination. Techniques like scaling are explored. Language use, such as "for every red, there are yellow", is modelled. Challenges include changing the common term in ratios.
SS5_desc_short: Students enhance their ratio understanding in algebraic contexts. They learn that in ratio a:b, a+b is the total. Bar models link worded and algebraic ratio problems. They explore algebraic ratios involving differences, like finding x and y values in x:y=2:3, y-x=8, and combining or fractioning algebraic ratios.
SS6_desc_short: Students learn to form and solve equations using equivalent ratios, starting with 1:1 algebraic ratios, then unitary ratios like 2x:20=1:2, emphasizing 2x≠20. They explore relationships in ratios, manipulate expressions to create equations, and use bar models for support. Advanced students may tackle non-unitary ratios and quadratic equations.
SS7_desc_short: Students link map scales to ratios, ensuring confidence in metric conversions, like centimetres to metres. Emphasize same-unit ratios; 1 cm to 3 m can't be 1:3 due to different units. Teach conversion strategies, like centimetres to metres before kilometres. Encourage interpreting scale diagrams, linking map distances to actual distances.

Small Step 8: Add and subtract more complex algebraic fractions (E)
Small Step 9: Multiply and divide more complex algebraic fractions (E)
SS1_desc: In this small step, students add and subtract fractions, building on prior learning from Key Stage 3. Students may need a reminder of the procedure to add and subtract a pair of fractions, including those with different denominators, using equivalence to find a common denominator. This extends to adding and subtracting mixed numbers, requiring conversion between improper fractions and mixed numbers. Pictorial representations such as bar models or partitioned shapes can help deepen understanding, particularly with mixed numbers and finding common denominators. For contextual problems, it is useful to consider the approach used with integers and then apply it to fractions. Estimation should be encouraged to check the validity of answers, and verifying accuracy with a calculator will also strengthen their skills.
SS2_desc: In this small step, students recap and extend their knowledge of multiplying and dividing proper fractions and mixed numbers from Key Stage 3. Recapping how to find the reciprocal of a number can be helpful before reviewing the procedures for multiplying and dividing fractions. Pictorial representations, such as area models or partitioned shapes, remain useful for deepening understanding of the differences between these procedures. Encourage students to determine whether a problem requires multiplication or division, initially starting with similar problems involving integers before moving onto fractions. Students can also improve their calculator proficiency by checking their solutions.
SS3_desc: In this small step, students focus on identifying the correct procedure to solve problems involving fractions. They will select and perform the appropriate operations needed to solve each problem. Pictorial representations can support students in visualising the operations and verifying their reasoning. Initially, showing similar problems involving integers before introducing fractions can help students develop a solid understand of the correct calculation process. Encourage students to identify key words and phrases in word problems, such as “shared equally” for division, to guide their choice of operation. Include solving equations within this step to allow students to practise using their understanding of operations with fractions within abstract structures. Checking their answers with a calculator will help build both confidence and calculator proficiency.
SS4_desc: In this small step, students learn to apply the generalisations for adding and subtracting fractions to algebraic fractions with single terms. Some students may have encountered addition and subtraction of simple algebraic fractions as an extend step in Key Stage 3. Students may need reminding of simplifying algebraic terms, for example, x × x­ æ x2 and 2y + 3y æ 5y. Start with examples that have a common denominator, both numerical and algebraic, to build familiarity and confidence. As students’ confidence increases, progress to examples without common denominators, where students rewrite the fractions by finding equivalent algebraic fractions. Encourage students to simplify their final answers where possible.
SS5_desc: In this small step, students learn how to apply the generalisations for multiplying fractions with algebraic fractions. Some students may have encountered multiplying and dividing simple algebraic fractions as an extend step in Year 8. Students may need reminding of simplifying algebraic terms, for example, a × c æ ac and 2x × x æ 2x2 Begin by finding the product of a numerical fraction and integer, for example, 2 3 × 4 = 8 3 and compare with an algebraic example, such as x 3 × 4 æ 4 3 x . Use a similar approach with the product of two fractions, for example, 2 3 × 4 5 = (2 × 4) (3 × 5) = 8 15 and a b × c d æ ( × ) ( × ) a c b d æ ac bd . As students’ confidence develops, introduce examples that can be simplified. It can be helpful to remind students to check for common factors in the numerators and denominators before multiplying as this can make the process more efficient.
SS6_desc: In this small step, students apply the generalisations for dividing fractions to algebraic fractions with single terms. Some students may have been introduced to dividing simple algebraic fractions as an extend step in Year 8. Students may need a reminder of writing the reciprocal of a number before making comparisons with writing the reciprocal of an algebraic expression. Begin by dividing a fraction by an integer and explore the similarities and differences with an algebraic case, for example, 2 3 ÷ 5 = 2 15 and x 3 ÷ 5 = x 15 . Apply the generalisations further with the division of two fractions, for example, 2 5 ÷ 3 4 = 2 5 × 4 3 = 8 15 and a b ÷ c d = a b × d c = ad bc . As students’ confidence develops, introduce terms that require simplifying. It can be helpful to remind students to check for common factors in the numerators and denominators before calculating as this can make the process more efficient.
SS7_desc: In this small step, students simplify algebraic fractions where one or both the numerator and denominator require factorising. You may wish to discuss the use of the term “cancelling” when simplifying algebraic fractions, as it implies that factors are simply erased rather than simplified through division. Begin with fractions expressed in a factorised form such as 3( + 2) 2( + 2) x x and ( + 1)( + 2) ( + 2) x x x and encourage students to identify common factors that can be simplified through division. It can be helpful to recap factorising linear and quadratic expressions before introducing fractions that require factorising such as x 8 – 4 12 and 3 + 6 9 + 3 2 x x . As students develop confidence, progress to more complex algebraic fractions such as x x x 2 + 3 + 2 + 2 and x x x 2 2 – 4 – 5 + 6 before showing algebraic fractions with varying coefficients of x2
SS8_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding of adding and subtracting simple algebraic fractions to more complex expressions such as x 2 – 2 + 3 + 3 x Ensure students are confident with expanding and simplifying brackets. It can be helpful to first recap addition and subtraction of numerical fractions with common denominators, before introducing algebraic fractions with the same denominator, such as x x 3 – 5 – x x – 5, to reinforce the process of combining like terms in the numerators. As students’ confidence develops, introduce examples that require finding a common denominator, such as 1 x + x 1 – 1 and x x 2 – 2 + 3 + 3 x x Highlight the importance of rewriting each fraction using equivalent fractions before combining them. Encourage students to look for opportunities to factorise and simplify further after combining fractions.
SS9_desc: In this small step, students extend their understanding of multiplying and dividing algebraic fractions to more complex problems, such as 5 + 3 2 x x ÷ x2 15 – 9 Begin with examples such as a 3 × a + 4 2 before introducing examples with common factors such as 8 3 × a – 5 4 . Encourage students to check for common factors in the numerators/ denominators before multiplying to make the process more efficient. When dividing, reinforce the concept of multiplying by the reciprocal. As students’ confidence develops, introduce examples such as x x 2 2 – 4 – 16 × x x 3 – 12 + 2 , highlighting how factorisation helps simplify both before and after multiplication.
SS10_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding of solving fractional equations from earlier in the year to solve equations such as 4 + 6 x + 3 + 1 x = 1 Only quadratics that factorise will be covered in this step, though students may choose to use alternative methods. Start with simpler linear equations, such as c 2 + c 3 = 10 to reinforce finding a common denominator and combining fractions before solving, before moving onto examples such as x 2 – 1 9 = x – 2 3 . As their confidence develops, progress to more complex quadratic equations that require expanding and factorising, such as 4 + 6 x + 3 + 1 x = 1 Emphasise the importance of combining fractions, rearranging the equation to equal zero and fully simplifying before solving. Reinforce checking solutions by substituting them back into the original equation.
SS1_desc_short: Students build on Key Stage 3 knowledge to add and subtract fractions, including those with varied denominators, using equivalence for a common denominator. This extends to mixed numbers, requiring conversion between improper fractions. Pictorial models aid understanding. Estimation and calculator checks verify answers, reinforcing skills.
SS2_desc_short: Students extend Key Stage 3 knowledge on multiplying and dividing proper fractions and mixed numbers. They review finding reciprocals and differentiate between multiplication and division procedures using pictorial representations like area models. Initial focus is on integer problems, progressing to fractions. Calculator proficiency is improved by solution checking.
SS3_desc_short: Students learn to solve fraction problems by identifying correct procedures and performing operations. Pictorial aids and initial integer problems help visualise processes. Keywords in word problems guide operation choice. Practice includes solving equations and using calculators to verify answers, building confidence and proficiency.
SS4_desc_short: Students apply rules for adding and subtracting fractions to single-term algebraic fractions. This builds on Key Stage 3 knowledge of simple algebraic fractions and simplifying terms (e.g., x × x = x2, 2y + 3y = 5y). They start with common denominators, progressing to finding equivalent algebraic fractions. Simplification of final answers is encouraged.
SS5_desc_short: Students learn to multiply algebraic fractions, building on prior knowledge from Year 8. They practice simplifying algebraic terms and multiplying numerical fractions with integers and other fractions. As confidence grows, they tackle simplifiable examples, checking for common factors in numerators and denominators for efficiency.
SS6_desc_short: Students apply rules for dividing fractions to single-term algebraic fractions, comparing dividing a fraction by an integer to an algebraic case. They further explore dividing two fractions. As confidence grows, they simplify terms, checking for common factors in numerators and denominators to increase efficiency.
SS7_desc_short: Students simplify algebraic fractions requiring factorisation in the numerator, denominator, or both. The term "cancelling" is used to describe simplifying factors through division. Students identify common factors in factorised fractions, recap factorising linear and quadratic expressions, and progress to complex algebraic fractions with varying coefficients.
SS8_desc_short: Students enhance their skills in adding and subtracting complex algebraic fractions, expanding and simplifying brackets. They recap numerical fractions with common denominators before moving to algebraic fractions. They learn to combine like terms, find common denominators, rewrite fractions, and factorise and simplify after combining fractions.
SS9_desc_short: Students enhance their skills in multiplying and dividing complex algebraic fractions. They learn to identify common factors in numerators/denominators for efficient calculations and understand division as multiplication by the reciprocal. As confidence grows, factorisation is introduced to simplify equations before and after multiplication.
SS10_desc_short: Students build on solving fractional equations, starting with simpler linear equations to reinforce finding common denominators and combining fractions. Progress to quadratic equations that factorise, requiring expansion and simplification. Emphasise equation rearrangement to zero, full simplification, and solution checking by substituting back into the original equation.

Small Step 1: Place value for integers and decimals
Small Step 3: Add and subtract integers and decimals
Small Step 4: Multiply and divide integers and decimals
SS1_desc: In this small step, students consolidate their understanding of place value from Key Stage 3. Ensure students can represent integers and decimals on place value charts, highlighting the significance of the columns that represent a digit’s place value. Encourage the use of vocabulary such as “tens”, “tenths” and “hundredths” throughout, as well as their numerical equivalents: “10”, “ 1 10 ”, “ 1 100 ” and so on. This can be useful when highlighting the place value of specific digits in a given number, for example understanding that the number 4032 has 3 tens or a value of 30 Encourage students to focus on the structure of a number, for example saying 2.45 as “two point four five” rather than “two point forty-five”, which can lead to misconceptions such as 2.45 > 2.5
SS2_desc: In this small step, students use their understanding of place value to compare and order numbers. Encourage students to use inequality notation when comparing two numbers, for example 2.7 > 2.07. Writing digits of numbers in a place value chart can be useful to help students order numbers. Model how to systematically check each place value column, starting from the left with the greatest place value, to compare and order numbers where there are many digits in common, for example 4.317 and 4.173. Once students are confident ordering positive integers and decimals, they could order negative numbers or a combination of fractions, decimals or percentages. Using numbers in context, such as lengths, costs and masses, may be useful.
SS3_desc: In this small step, students revisit strategies to add and subtract integers and decimals covered in Key Stage 3. Remind students of situations where an exchange is needed, for example, when the sum of two digits in the same place value column is greater than 9 Comparing strategies to add or subtract integers and decimals with the same digits can support depth of understanding. For example, comparing 234 – 19 with 23.4 – 1.9 To develop fluency, students should explore different methods depending on the problem, such as rewriting 1000 – 317 as 999 – 316, where the latter calculation does not need exchanges but it gives the same result. If appropriate, students could be encouraged to apply their understanding to solving one-step equations without the use of a calculator, for example, x + 2.92 = 5.8
SS4_desc: In this small step, students revisit strategies to multiply and divide integers and decimals from Key Stage 3. Explore different strategies for multiplying numbers, for example, using an area model or a column method. Begin with multiplying numbers by a single digit and, as confidence develops, move onto multiplying by two-digit numbers and greater. Encourage students to represent remainders as decimals when dividing integers. Compare similar calculations when multiplying or dividing with decimals, such as comparing 928 × 4 and 92.8 × 4 or 327 ÷ 8 and 32.7 ÷ 8, and encourage students to estimate an answer before calculating.
SS5_desc: In this small step, students revisit adding, subtracting, multiplying and dividing with directed number covered in Key Stage 3. Number lines are a useful representation to help students complete calculations that cross zero, for example, 4 – 9 or –2 + 5. Double sided counters could be used to model addition or subtraction of directed number, ensuring students are reminded of the concept of a “zero pair”. Arrays of counters could also be used to establish multiplication and division facts involving negative numbers, for example, if –12 ÷ 3 = –4 then –12 ÷ –4 = 3 If appropriate, students can also explore using the four operations with negative decimals or solving equations involving negative solutions and/or operations.
SS6_desc: In this small step, students develop their understanding of how to priortise operations in calculations, including those with directed number. Acronyms such as BIDMAS or BODMAS may be misleading as they imply that each operation has its own place in priority. Instead, highlight that some operations have equal priority, for example multiplication and division. Draw students’ attention to examples where operations should be calculated from left to right, for example, when calculating 20 – 5 + 3, 20 – 5 should be calculated first. Ensure students have opportunities to explore calculations involving all four operations, as well as powers and brackets throughout the step. Most calculators use order of operations correctly, but comparing and checking answers is useful.
SS7_desc: In this small step, students use known numerical facts to derive other related facts. Students will make links between similar calculations, for example, understanding that if 12 × 9 = 108 then 1.2 × 0.9 = 1.08. Attention should be drawn to how the numbers in a calculation have been manipulated and the effect this will have on a given answer. For example, if both numbers in a multiplication are multiplied by 10, then the new product will be 100 times greater than the original product. Students must also understand that this logic does not apply to division. For example, if both numbers in a division are multiplied by 10, then the new product will be the same as the original product. For division, this can be linked to students’ understanding of equivalent fractions, for example, 45 ÷ 0.5 = 45 0.5 = 90 1 = 90
SS8_desc: In this small step, students consolidate the learning from this block so far, applying their knowledge to reasoning and problem- solving contexts. Students interpret questions and identify an appropriate approach to solve a variety of multi-step problems. Students may need to be reminded of key vocabulary used in different contexts, for example, “deposit” or “instalments” in financial contexts. It may also be appropriate to scaffold problems initially, so that students can gain confidence and construct a strategy or plan to solve the rest of the problem. Bar models, among other representations, can be used to help students visualise the problem and support their decisions around which calculations to perform. Consider the use of “goal-free” or “slow reveal” problems to build students’ confidence further. As a non-calculator step, this step offers students a valuable opportunity to reinforce and practise their arithmetic skills.
SS1_desc_short: Students reinforce Key Stage 3 place value understanding, representing integers and decimals on charts. Emphasize column significance, using terms like "tens", "tenths", "hundredths", and their numerical equivalents. Highlight specific digit values in numbers, and promote correct number structure to avoid misconceptions.
SS2_desc_short: Students learn to compare and order numbers using place value and inequality notation. Techniques include writing numbers in a place value chart and checking each column from left to right. Once confident with positive integers and decimals, they progress to negative numbers, fractions, decimals, and percentages in real-world contexts.
SS3_desc_short: Students review adding and subtracting integers and decimals from Key Stage 3, focusing on situations requiring an exchange. They compare strategies for similar digits, develop fluency through varied methods like rewriting equations, and apply understanding to solve one-step equations without calculators.
SS4_desc_short: Students review Key Stage 3 strategies for multiplying and dividing integers and decimals. They start with single-digit multiplication, progressing to two-digit numbers. Strategies include area models and column methods. Remainders are represented as decimals in division. Students compare calculations with decimals and estimate answers before solving.
SS5_desc_short: Students review adding, subtracting, multiplying, and dividing with directed numbers from Key Stage 3. Number lines and double-sided counters aid in calculations crossing zero, reinforcing the "zero pair" concept. Arrays establish multiplication and division with negatives. If suitable, operations with negative decimals and solving negative equations are explored.
SS6_desc_short: Students learn to prioritize operations in calculations, including directed numbers. Emphasize that some operations, like multiplication and division, have equal priority and should be calculated left to right. Provide opportunities to explore all operations, powers, and brackets. Encourage checking answers, as calculators correctly follow operation order.
SS7_desc_short: Students learn to derive new facts from known numerical ones, linking similar calculations. They'll understand the effects of manipulating numbers in multiplication and division, noting that multiplying both numbers by 10 yields different results in each operation. This concept is connected to understanding equivalent fractions.
SS8_desc_short: Students apply learned knowledge to reasoning and problem-solving tasks, interpreting questions and identifying solutions for multi-step problems. Key vocabulary, scaffolding, and visual aids like bar models are used for support. "Goal-free" or "slow reveal" problems help build confidence and reinforce arithmetic skills without calculators.

Small Step 2: Find solutions to equations using straight lines graphs
Small Step 5: Find the equation of a line from a graph (E)
Small Step 6: Find the midpoint of a line segment
Small Step 7: Equation of a straight-line graph given one point and a gradient
Small Step 8: Equation of a straight-line graph given two points (E)
SS1_desc: In this small step, students build on their knowledge of plotting straight line graphs from Key Stage 3. Remind students how to draw the lines y = x, y = –x and lines that are parallel to the axes. Draw attention to important features, for example, the x- and y-coordinates are equal for any point that lies on the line y = x. Model how to complete a table of values to generate pairs of coordinates before progressing to questions where a table of values is not given. If appropriate, students should have opportunities to explore forms other than y = mx + c, such as x + y = 10 Encourage students to discuss whether a point lies on a given line by substituting the coordinates into the equation.
SS2_desc: In this small step, students use straight-line graphs to solve related equations. Remind students how to identify points that lie on pairs of lines, for example, how to verify the point (2, 6) lies on the lines y = 3x and y = 6. Build on this to show students how to solve an equation graphically. For example, solving 2x – 7 = 8 by drawing the graphs of y = 2x – 7 and y = 8 and identifying the x-coordinate of the point of intersection as the solution to the equation. As students’ confidence builds, provide more-complex equations to solve graphically, such as 2x + 3 = x + 1 Dynamic graphing software is recommended to support understanding.
SS3_desc: In this small step, students explore the gradients of straight line graphs and learn that the value of m in the equation y = mx + c represents the gradient. For students previously following the support scheme, this may be their first formal introduction. Explain that gradient measures the steepness of a line, using the formula gradient = change in change in y x Start with lines on a grid, then progress to coordinate axes. Begin with positive gradients before introducing negative and fractional gradients. Encourage students to observe how changing the gradient affects steepness and highlight that lines with the same gradient are parallel. Dynamic graphing software can effectively support visual understanding.
SS4_desc: In this small step, students build on learning from previous steps and Key Stage 3 to interpret equations of a line given in the form y = mx + c, identifying both the gradient, m, and the coordinates of the y-intercept, (0, c). Students who have exclusively followed the support scheme in Key Stage 3 may not be familiar with the y-intercept. Students also build on their understanding that lines with the same gradient are parallel and use this knowledge to write equations of parallel lines. Once students can identify the gradient and y-intercept, introduce non-standard examples such as y = 3 + 5x or 2x + 1 = y. Students could also explore equations of lines that need to be rearranged into the form of y = mx + c, such as 2y = 4x + 6
SS5_desc: In this small step, students identify gradients and y-intercepts from equations, before writing the equation of a straight line from a graph. Begin by giving students structured practice in identifying gradients and y-intercepts separately, before combining these to write equations of straight lines. Remind students that m represents the gradient and c represents the y-intercept within y = mx + c. Introduce non-standard forms such as y = 6 – 2x, and support students in rearranging these into the form y = mx + c. Use examples with different axis scales to develop fluency. Once confident, students should find the equation of a line from a graph by calculating the gradient from two points and identifying the y-intercept. Challenge students by working with fractional and negative values.
SS6_desc: In this small step, students build on their knowledge of coordinates to find the midpoint of a line segment. Students may have previously explored this in Year 8 as an extend step. Begin by encouraging students to find the midpoint of two numbers on a number line, before transferring this skill to working in two dimensions. Students will learn that the mean of the x- and y-coordinates from a pair of points will give the x- and y-coordinates of the midpoint. Begin by modelling examples with line segments presented on a grid before students progress to working abstractly with two coordinates. If appropriate, students could work out the coordinates of the start or end point of a line segment given the midpoint. Challenge students to solve more complex problems involving midpoints and 2-D shapes.
SS7_desc: In this small step, students combine their knowledge from previous steps to find the equation of a line given one point and a gradient. Begin with examples where the point given is the y-intercept, such as (0, 4). It may be helpful to spend time recapping with students how the x- and y-coordinates relate to an equation of a line. Once confidence is built, introduce questions where a point other than the y-intercept is given. Encourage the understanding that, by substituting the given values of m, x and y into the equation y = mx + c, then the value of c can be calculated. Some students may benefit from a reminder of methods for solving two-step equations before this step. If appropriate, students could find equations of parallel lines passing through a specific point.
SS8_desc: In this small step, students build on their knowledge of straight line graphs to find the equation of a line given two points. Begin by calculating the gradient of a line given the coordinates of two points it passes through. Once students are confident with this, make links to the previous step where students used the gradient and single point to calculate the y-intercept. Initially, use examples where the gradients and coordinates are positive, before introducing more difficult examples where the gradient is negative. Discuss that either of the given points can be chosen for the substitution stage of working. Sketching graphs can be a helpful scaffold for students who require further support.
SS9_desc: In this small step, students apply their understanding of gradients and intercepts to real-life linear graphs. This step focuses on graphs that do not show direct proportion. Encourage students to interpret the intercept of the vertical axis, for example, in showing a fixed cost for a taxi fare. Discuss with students how the gradient represents the rate of increase or decrease per unit on the horizontal axis, such as cost per mile. Where appropriate, support students in linking contextual graphs to equations, allowing them to explore how changes to the gradient or intercept of the horizontal axis affect the relationship represented.
SS1_desc_short: Students build on Key Stage 3 knowledge by plotting straight line graphs, drawing lines y = x, y = –x, and parallel lines. They learn to generate coordinate pairs from tables of values and explore equations beyond y = mx + c, like x + y = 10. They also determine if a point lies on a line by substituting coordinates.
SS2_desc_short: Students use straight-line graphs to solve equations and identify intersecting points. They learn to verify points on lines, like (2, 6) on y = 3x and y = 6, and solve equations graphically, such as 2x – 7 = 8. As confidence grows, they tackle complex equations. Dynamic graphing software aids understanding.
SS3_desc_short: Students learn that 'm' in y=mx+c represents a graph's gradient. They explore gradients on straight line graphs, starting with grid lines, then coordinate axes. They study positive, negative, and fractional gradients, observing how gradient changes affect steepness. They learn parallel lines have the same gradient. Dynamic graphing software supports visual understanding.
SS4_desc_short: Students build on prior knowledge to interpret line equations in the form y = mx + c, identifying the gradient (m) and y-intercept (0, c). They understand parallel lines share the same gradient and write corresponding equations. They also explore non-standard examples and equations needing rearrangement.
SS5_desc_short: Students identify gradients and y-intercepts from equations and write straight line equations from graphs. They practice identifying gradients and y-intercepts, then combine these skills. They learn that m is the gradient and c is the y-intercept in y = mx + c, rearrange non-standard forms, and work with different axis scales.
SS6_desc_short: Students build on coordinate knowledge to find a line segment's midpoint, initially on a number line then in two dimensions. They learn the mean of x and y coordinates from two points gives the midpoint. They start with grid examples, progress to abstract coordinates, and solve complex midpoint problems.
SS7_desc_short: Students apply prior knowledge to find a line's equation using a point and gradient. Starting with the y-intercept as the point, they relate x and y coordinates to the equation. They then substitute m, x, and y values into y = mx + c to find c. This may include solving two-step equations and finding parallel line equations.
SS8_desc_short: Students expand on straight line graph knowledge by finding line equations from two points. They calculate the line's gradient using two points, then determine the y-intercept using the gradient and a point. Initially, examples use positive gradients and coordinates, then introduce negative gradients. Either point can be used for substitution. Sketching supports understanding.
SS9_desc_short: Students apply knowledge of gradients and intercepts to real-life linear graphs, interpreting the vertical axis intercept and gradient's rate of change. They explore non-proportional graphs, like taxi fare costs, and connect graphs to equations, examining how altering the gradient or intercept affects the represented relationship.

Small Step 2: Use the property that probabilities sum to 1
Small Step 5: Sample spaces for 1 or more events
SS1_desc: In this small step, students revisit prior learning from Key Stage 3, focusing on calculating the probability of a single event. Emphasise that probability measures the likelihood of an outcome, and it is always expressed as a value between 0 and 1, where 0 represents an impossible event and 1 represents a certain event. Remind students that probabilities can be written as fractions, decimals or percentages. Students should also be familiar with probability notation, such as P(red), which represents the probability of selecting a red counter in a given context. Mutually exclusive events, such as rolling a 2 or a 6 on a dice should also be discussed.
SS2_desc: Building on the previous small step, students apply the idea that the sum of probabilities for all mutually exclusive events sums to 1 Begin with examples where the total is given before moving on to examples where the probability of the event occurring is given. Reinforce that if the probability of an event is known, the probability of it not occurring can be found by subtracting this value from 1 Students should practise applying this property when working with probabilities expressed as fractions, decimals or percentages and they should be confident in converting between the forms. Highlight to students that this relationship can be generalised by P(A) + P(not A) = 1 Students should use calculators to reduce cognitive load if required.
SS3_desc: In this small step, students work systematically to list all possible outcomes in a given situation. Start with simple examples, such as flipping a coin or rolling a dice, before progressing to more complex examples. Encourage students to explain how they know their list is complete and that no outcomes have been missed. Model systematic approaches, such as changing one element at a time to ensure accuracy. Encourage students to discuss whether the order of outcomes matters in the given context and remind them to check that their lists do not include repeated outcomes. At this stage, sample space diagrams are not required for organising outcomes, as they will be explored later in the block.
SS4_desc: In this small step, students consolidate their understanding from Key Stage 3, to consider relative frequency as a form of experimental probability. Students should recognise that probability is often considered in theoretical terms, where predictions are made about events that have not yet happened, while relative frequency is based on data from experiments or past events. Comparing theoretical probability with relative frequency can strengthen students’ understanding. A key discussion point is the effect of the number of trials on accuracy. Students should see that as the number of trials increases, the relative frequency tends to approach the theoretical probability. Students will also estimate the expected number of outcomes of an event using the relative frequency.
SS5_desc: In this small step, students construct sample space diagrams for one or more events and use these sample spaces to calculate theoretical probabilities. This step revisits learning from Key Stage 3 and provides an opportunity to incorporate their skills from earlier in the block, using a systematic method when setting up a sample space diagram. Discuss with students the pros and cons of using a list or a grid, and how a grid can organise work. When calculating probabilities, explore the importance of the order of outcomes and whether the different options are equally likely.
SS6_desc: This small step offers an opportunity to revisit and extend learning from Key Stage 3 to complete and interpret. Students should be able to design and construct two-way tables to represent data. They begin with partially completed tables before progressing to filling in or creating a full table from given information. When working with two-way tables, students also interpret probabilities from them. Particular attention should be paid to the wording of probability questions, so that students can recognise whether their probability is calculated from the whole group or from a specific subgroup. Reinforce accurate probability notation and remind students to express their answers as fractions, decimals or percentages.
SS7_desc: In this small step, students revisit and extend learning from Key Stage 3 to use frequency trees to calculate probabilities. They should be able to construct a frequency tree from given data, though some time may be needed to consolidate this skill before moving on to calculating probabilities. When working with probabilities, it is useful to discuss the similarities and differences between frequency trees and sample space diagrams in how they represent and support problem- solving. Be clear about the distinction between finding the probability from the whole group versus from a given condition, such as “given that the person is in Year 10”.
SS8_desc: In this small step, students revisit independent events, first introduced in Key Stage 3. Distinguishing between independent and dependent events can be a challenging concept, so it is important to spend time ensuring students can recognise the difference. Independent events are those where the occurrence of one event does not affect the probability of the other. Once confident in identifying independent events, students apply the multiplication rule, P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B), to calculate the probability of two or more independent events occurring. This can then be extended by using the rule to test whether two events are independent.
SS9_desc: In this small step, students are introduced to tree diagrams for the first time, with a focus on independent events, building on the previous step. Explain that tree diagrams are another way of representing probability problems, alongside frequency trees and sample space diagrams, but that tree diagrams use probabilities on the branches rather than frequencies or totals. Students apply the multiplication rule, P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B) to calculate probabilities along the branches, and will also be introduced to the addition rule, P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B), to find the probability of one event or another occurring.
SS10_desc: This extend step builds on the previous small step by introducing probabilities for dependent events. Highlight to students that each scenario must be considered to calculate the probabilities on the second set of branches. A common example is “without replacement”, where modelling helps students see how one event affects another. For example, selecting a red counter from a bag and not replacing it changes both the total number of counters and the number of red counters for the next branch. Once the tree diagram is constructed, students should recognise that the calculations are carried out in the same way as in the previous step, using both the multiplication rule and the addition rule.
SS1_desc_short: Students review Key Stage 3 concepts, focusing on calculating single event probability, expressed as a value between 0 (impossible) and 1 (certain). They should understand probabilities as fractions, decimals, percentages, and notation like P(red). Discussion includes mutually exclusive events, like rolling a 2 or 6 on dice.
SS2_desc_short: Students learn that the total probability of all exclusive events equals 1. They practice calculating the probability of an event not occurring by subtracting its occurrence probability from 1. This concept is applied to probabilities as fractions, decimals, or percentages, using calculators if needed. P(A) + P(not A) = 1.
SS3_desc_short: Students systematically list all outcomes for scenarios, starting with simple ones like coin flips or dice rolls.
