Students should also use scaling strategies to solve problems. For example, if 4 rulers cost £5 then the cost of 8 rulers can be found by multiplying the cost of 4 rulers by 2. Students will work algebraically with direct proportion later in this block.
SS3_desc: In this small step, students will solve financial problems, building on prior learning from Key Stage 3. Provide students opportunities to explore different strategies to compare items and cost, for example, scaling to compare the same amount of each item or working out the unit cost of each item. It is important to discuss when different strategies may be more efficient than others. Students should also be able to solve problems with common everyday life special offers, for example, “20% off’” or “3 for 2” offers. The use of a calculator is recommended for this step to place more emphasis on strategies to solve problems rather than mental arithmetic.
SS4_desc: In this small step, students draw and interpret conversion graphs to complete approximate conversions. Explain to students that if a conversion graph forms a straight line that passes through the origin, it represents a direct proportion. Encourage students to draw a line segment to the graph when reading a value from the axes. Students can also use the learning from the previous step to work out values that are not in the range of the data represented on a graph. For example, if a graph shows that 5 cm ≈ 2 inches, then 50 cm ≈ 20 inches. Unfamiliar contexts, such as converting metric units to imperial units, are useful to encourage students to use a graph for their conversions.
SS5_desc: In this small step, students will revisit exchange rates, which were covered in Key Stage 3. Explain what an exchange rate implies, for example, £1 = €1.30 means every £1 is worth €1.30 Model strategies to highlight the directly proportional relationship between two values. For example, if the number of euros is found by multiplying the number of pounds by 1.30, then the number of pounds can be found by dividing the number of euros by 1.30 Double number lines can be a useful representation to support students. Ensure that students practise with a variety of currencies. Everyday life contexts are also recommended, for example, comparing prices of items in different countries.
SS6_desc: In this small step, students explore the relationship between quantities that are inversely proportional. Students should understand that if two quantities are inversely proportional, then when one amount increases, the other amount decreases at the same rate. Students’ attention should be drawn to the fact that the product of the two quantities is constant as the quantities vary. It may be useful to begin with a scenario involving one person, such as 1 person takes 4 hours to paint a fence, before moving on to more challenging problems. Encourage students to think about whether a task will take more or less time with more people. Throughout the step, discuss any assumptions that have been made, such as assuming every person works at the same rate. Foundation students do not need to describe inverse proportion relationships algebraically, for example, y k x =
SS7_desc: In this small step, students will use their understanding of direct proportion to form equations. Students will be introduced to the symbol ∝, which is used to denote when two variables are proportional. For example, y ∝ x means y is proportional to x, which implies direct proportion. Highlight that direct proportion equations are written in the form y = kx, where x and y are variables and k is the constant of proportionality. Encourage students to substitute known information into their formula to work out the constant of proportionality. They will then use this constant to work out different corresponding values of the variables. Students can also solve problems in context. For example, the cost, c, of pens is directly proportional to the number of pens, p.
SS8_desc: In this small step, students will plot and interpret graphical representations of directly and inversely proportional relationships, which they may have covered in Year 9. Remind students that direct proportion graphs form a straight line from the origin. Highlight that inversely proportional relationships produce a non-linear graph where the horizontal and vertical axes are asymptotes. When working with direct proportion graphs, ensure students can use proportional reasoning to complete conversions outside of the range of values of the graph. Provide students with plenty of opportunities to identify whether a graph represents a directly proportional or an inversely proportional relationship.
SS1_desc_short: Students revisit ratio problems from Year 10, learning to share amounts in a ratio from a total, part, or difference. They use bar models to identify problem differences and establish solution strategies. Links between fractions and ratios are explored. "Combine ratios" and "link ratio and algebra" may be new steps.
SS2_desc_short: Students will learn direct proportion through solving problems, understanding it as a multiplicative relationship between two values. They'll use double number lines, ratio tables, unit cost calculations, inverse operations, and scaling strategies. This prepares them for algebraic work with direct proportion.
SS3_desc_short: Students build on Key Stage 3 learning to solve financial problems, comparing item costs using strategies like scaling or calculating unit cost. They should understand when different strategies are efficient and solve everyday special offers problems (e.g., "20% off", "3 for 2"). Calculator use is recommended.
SS4_desc_short: Students learn to draw and interpret conversion graphs for approximate conversions, understanding that a straight line through the origin indicates direct proportion. They use prior knowledge to calculate values outside the graph's range, applying this in unfamiliar contexts like metric to imperial unit conversions.
SS5_desc_short: Students revisit exchange rates from Key Stage 3, understanding that £1 = €1.30 signifies each £1 is worth €1.30. They learn the proportional relationship between currencies, using multiplication and division. Double number lines aid this process. Practice involves various currencies and real-life price comparisons.
SS6_desc_short: Students explore inverse proportionality, understanding that when one quantity increases, the other decreases at the same rate, keeping the product constant. They apply this concept to real-life scenarios, like task completion time varying with people involved, while considering assumptions like consistent work rates. Algebraic descriptions aren't required for foundation students.
SS7_desc_short: Students learn direct proportion, symbolised as ∝, forming equations like y = kx, where k is a constant. They substitute known data to find k, then use it to calculate variable values. This concept applies to real-world problems, like cost (c) being proportional to quantity (p) of pens.
SS8_desc_short: Students will plot and interpret graphs of direct and inverse proportions, previously covered in Year 9. They'll learn that direct proportions form straight lines from the origin, while inverse proportions create non-linear graphs with axes as asymptotes. They'll apply proportional reasoning to conversions and identify graph types.

SS1_desc: In this small step, students draw and interpret 3-D shapes on isometric paper. Students may be familiar with isometric paper when exploring volumes in cuboids in Key Stage 2. Ensure students know that adjacent dots on isometric paper are equidistant. Start by having students draw cubes and cuboids isometrically made from centimetre cubes, before drawing cubes and cuboids with labelled integer dimensions. Encourage students to start drawing the shape from its base towards the bottom of the isometric paper, so that they do not run out of room. When ready, have students practise drawing and interpreting triangular prisms isometrically. Having 3-D shapes available will help students to understand how the shape is represented using the isometric paper. Volume of cubes, cuboids and triangular prisms can be interleaved in this step.
SS2_desc: In this small step, students will draw accurate and scaled plans as well as front and side elevations of 3-D shapes. They should begin by drawing plans and elevations of cubes and cuboids before moving onto more complex shapes such as prisms. Allowing students access to physical objects such as interlocking cubes to make shapes or dynamic geometry software may be useful to help students visualise objects before attempting to draw plans and elevations. If appropriate, have students draw or describe shapes when given the plan and elevation views. This includes being able to draw them on isometric paper, which was covered in the previous step.
SS3_desc: In this small step, students will build on prior learning from Year 10, combining their understanding of the area of a circle and the volume of prisms. Highlight that, although cylinders are not prisms, the volume of a cylinder is calculated in a similar way by multiplying the area of the constant cross-section by the length or height. Allow students the opportunity to practise giving answers exactly in terms of pi (π) and to various degrees of accuracy, for example, rounded to 1 decimal place. If appropriate, students could solve problems involving the volume of a cylinder, for example calculating an unknown length within the cylinder.
SS4_desc: In this small step, students use the formula to calculate the volume of a sphere. Tell students that the formula to work out the volume of a sphere with radius r is 4 3 πr3, and model how to input relevant calculations into a calculator, for example 4 3 × π × 53, to work out the volume of a sphere with a radius of 5 cm. As with other formulae involving pi (π), students should have the opportunity to provide exact answers in terms of pi (π), as well as giving approximate answers to varying degrees of accuracy. When students are comfortable with finding the volume of a sphere, they could begin to investigate the volume of hemispheres and compound shapes.
SS5_desc: In this small step, students calculate the volume of cones and pyramids. Students may not have covered this before, as this is an extend step in Year 9. When introducing the volume of a cone, it may be useful to draw out similarities with the formula for the volume of a cylinder. That is, the volume of a cone is 1 3 of the volume of a cylinder with the same height and radius. Then further similarity may be shown between the formulae for cones and pyramids. For example, the volume of both can be calculated with 1 3 × area of base × height. If appropriate, students could form and solve equations involving volumes, such as calculating the length of a radius when given the height and volume of a cone.
SS6_desc: In this small step, students will calculate the surface area of a sphere using the formula. Remind students that the surface area is the area that covers the outside of a 3-D shape, and introduce them to the formula Area = 4πr2. Although this formula is provided, it may be beneficial to investigate and compare the structure of area and volume formulae using dimensional analysis. For example, area is measured in square units and 4πr2 involves a length (a radius) being squared. If appropriate, students could also calculate the surface area of a hemisphere.
SS7_desc: In this small step, students will learn how to calculate the surface area of a cylinder, which they may have studied previously as an extension step in Year 9. Exploring the net of a cylinder allows students to see that the length of the curved surface area is equal to the circumference of the circular face. This then supports students to understand the calculations needed to work out the total surface area. As with previous steps, allow students the opportunity to practise giving answers exactly in terms of pi (π), and to various degrees of accuracy, such as rounded to 1 decimal place.
SS8_desc: In this small step, students will learn how to calculate the surface area of a pyramid. Note that students may have calculated the surface area of prisms in Year 10. Students will only need to consider pyramids where the lateral faces are congruent triangles and the apex of the pyramid is directly above the centre of the base. Nets can be a useful representation to highlight the calculations necessary to work out the surface area of a pyramid. Students need to pay particular attention to given dimensions, for example, ensuring they use the perpendicular height of a triangular face when calculating its area.
SS9_desc: In this small step, students will use a given formula to work out the area of the curved surface of a cone, as well as finding the total surface area. This will normally be given as A = πrl where A is the area of the curved surface, r is the radius of the cone and l is the slant height of the cone. Ensure students understand that the net of a cone consists of a circular base as well as another curved surface, and therefore the total surface area will be the sum of these areas. If appropriate, students could solve other problems involving cones, such as calculating the length of a radius when given the surface area and slant height of a cone.
SS10_desc: In this small step, students convert metric units of area. This may have been previously explored in the extend step in Year 9. Begin by exploring a square with a side length of 1 cm and comparing it to a square with a side length of 10 mm, highlighting that both lengths are equivalent. This leads to the key insight that 1 cm² is equal to 100 mm2 A similar approach can be used to compare other metric units, such as converting between m2 and cm2. Encourage students to recognise that the area scale factor is the square of the linear scale factor. Once students are confident, they can progress to converting between units in a single calculation for example, converting 3 cm2 to mm2
SS11_desc: In this small step, students convert metric units of volume. This may have been previously explored as an extend step in Year 9. Similar to the previous step, students start by comparing the volume of a cube with a side length of 1 cm and another cube with a side length of 10 mm. This supports students in understanding that 1 cm3 is equal to 1000 mm3 Links between other measurements of volume, such as m3 and cm3, should also be explored. Encourage students to recognise that the volume scale factor is the cube of the linear scale factor. Students can then progress to converting between units in a single calculation.
SS1_desc_short: Students draw and interpret 3-D shapes like cubes, cuboids, and triangular prisms on isometric paper, ensuring understanding of equidistant adjacent dots. They start with centimetre cubes, then move to labelled integer dimensions, always drawing from the base. Interleaving volume calculations enhances understanding of shape representation.
SS2_desc_short: Students will create accurate, scaled plans and elevations of 3-D shapes, starting with cubes and cuboids, then progressing to complex prisms. Using interlocking cubes or geometry software can aid visualization. They may also draw or describe shapes from given plans and elevations, including on isometric paper.
SS3_desc_short: Building on Year 10 knowledge, students will calculate cylinder volume similarly to prisms, by multiplying the constant cross-section area by height. Practice includes giving exact answers in terms of pi (π), rounding to 1 decimal place, and solving problems involving cylinder volume, like finding unknown lengths.
SS4_desc_short: Students learn to calculate sphere volume using the formula 4/3 πr³. They practice inputting calculations into a calculator, providing exact answers in terms of pi, and giving approximate answers. Once comfortable, they explore the volume of hemispheres and compound shapes.
SS5_desc_short: In this step, Year 9 students learn to calculate cone and pyramid volumes, drawing parallels with cylinder volume formulas. They understand that cone and pyramid volumes are 1/3 of the base area times height. They may also solve equations involving volumes, like finding a radius given height and volume.
SS6_desc_short: Students will calculate a sphere's surface area using the formula Area = 4πr2. They'll understand surface area as the covering of a 3D shape and compare area and volume formulae structures through dimensional analysis. The concept of area as square units and radius squaring will be explored.
SS7_desc_short: Students will calculate a cylinder's surface area, understanding that the curved surface length equals the circular face's circumference. They'll explore cylinder nets, practice calculations for total surface area, and provide answers in terms of pi (π), with varying degrees of accuracy, like rounding to one decimal place.
SS8_desc_short: Students learn to calculate the pyramid's surface area, considering only pyramids with congruent triangular lateral faces and a central apex. They may use nets to understand the calculations and must pay attention to dimensions, specifically the perpendicular height of a triangular face. Prior knowledge of prism surface area is helpful.
SS9_desc_short: Students will calculate the area of a cone's curved surface and total surface area using the formula A = πrl. They'll understand a cone's net includes a circular base and curved surface. They may also solve problems like finding the radius length given the surface area and slant height.
SS10_desc_short: Students convert metric units of area, comparing squares of 1 cm and 10 mm sides to understand 1 cm² equals 100 mm². They learn to convert between m² and cm², recognizing the area scale factor as the square of the linear scale factor. This enables single calculation conversions, like 3 cm² to mm².
SS11_desc_short: Students learn to convert metric volume units, building on prior knowledge from Year 9. They compare volumes of cubes with side lengths of 1cm and 10mm to understand that 1 cm3 equals 1000 mm3. They explore links between other volume measurements like m3 and cm3, and apply volume scale factors.

Small Step 2: Find unknown lengths and angles in similar shapes
Small Step 5: Prove a pair of triangles are similar (E)
SS1_desc: In this small step, students will revisit their understanding of similar shapes from Key Stage 3. Students will build on their knowledge of enlargement to identify similar shapes. Begin by encouraging students to calculate the scale factor of enlargement for each corresponding length and highlight that if the scale factor is the same, the shapes must be similar. Remind students that angles in corresponding positions in similar shapes are equal. Once students are confident in identifying similar shapes using scale factors, they can begin to incorporate ratios to determine if shapes are similar.
SS2_desc: In this small step, students calculate unknown angles and lengths in similar shapes, building on learning from Key Stage 3. Ensure students identify corresponding sides correctly, drawing attention to examples where the shapes have different orientations. Model strategies to calculate the scale factor of enlargement between the shapes, ensuring students can work with both integer and fractional scale factors, for example the scale factor from shape A to B and shape B to A. Students should be aware that corresponding angles in similar shapes are equal. Encourage the use of geometric notation when labelling side lengths and angles.
SS3_desc: In this small step, students apply their knowledge of angle facts to show that a pair of triangles are similar. Remind students of facts involving angles that are vertically opposite, angles that form a straight line and angles that exist within pairs of parallel lines. Ensure students see a variety of examples including triangles that share a vertex as well triangles embedded within each other, sharing a common angle. Support students to identify the corresponding vertices of two similar triangles. Highlight that if all three angles in one triangle are the same as the angles in another, then the triangles are similar. If necessary, it may be appropriate to encourage students to draw the triangles separately and in the same orientation before working out unknown lengths and angles. Students should also recognise that using side ratios is an equally valid method of establishing similarity.
SS4_desc: In this small step, students consolidate and extend their understanding of similar shapes. Begin with a mixture of questions from previous steps where students calculate scale factors between similar shapes, determining if two shapes are similar and calculating unknown lengths and angles. Once students are confident, include problems involving similar triangles. Demonstrate strategies to help visualise the problem, such as drawing any shapes with a common vertex or angle as two separate shapes in the same orientation. Students should be encouraged to discuss their approaches and reasoning when solving the problems. It may be appropriate to interleave other topic areas such as Pythagoras’ theorem or trigonometry.
SS5_desc: In this extend step, students explore general examples of similar triangles and use formal proofs to justify their similarity. They may need reminders about angle rules, such as those involving parallel lines, to help identify equal angles within a shape. Emphasise the importance of using correct geometric notation when labelling angles and lengths in their proofs. Demonstrate how to structure explanations clearly and precisely to strengthen their justifications. Reinforce that triangles are similar if all corresponding angles are equal or if their corresponding side lengths are proportional. Where appropriate, challenge students to apply Pythagoras’ theorem within similar right-angled triangles.
SS6_desc: In this small step, students explore the differences between similarity and congruence. Remind them that congruent shapes are identical, while similar shapes are proportional to each other. Students should compare different variations of a shape and categorise them to determine if they are congruent, similar or neither. Ensure students are exposed to shapes in different orientations to encourage them to consider corresponding lengths and angles. By reasoning in this way, students will have a better idea of where the concepts overlap and what characteristics are unique to each.
SS7_desc: In this small step, students will learn how to identify the properties of congruent triangles, which they may have covered in Year 9. It may be necessary to remind students of the conditions of congruence such as SSS, ASA, SAS and RHS. Begin by defining each condition of congruence, highlighting the key features and showing various examples and non-examples for each. Students should understand the minimum information needed to establish congruence between triangles. Ensure students see examples that are commonly mistaken as congruent but do not present enough information, for example, two triangles with three angles in common are similar, but more information would be needed to determine if the triangles are congruent.
SS1_desc_short: Students will review similar shapes from Key Stage 3, focusing on enlargement and identifying similar shapes through scale factors. They'll calculate scale factors for corresponding lengths, noting equal scale factors indicate similarity. They'll also compare angles in corresponding positions. Once confident, they'll use ratios to confirm shape similarity.
SS2_desc_short: Students calculate unknown angles and lengths in similar shapes, building on Key Stage 3 knowledge. Emphasize identifying corresponding sides, considering different orientations, and calculating enlargement scale factors (integer and fractional). Highlight that corresponding angles in similar shapes are equal. Encourage geometric notation for labelling.
SS3_desc_short: Students apply angle knowledge to identify similar triangles, using facts about vertically opposite, straight line, and parallel line angles. Examples include shared vertex and embedded triangles. Students should identify corresponding vertices, understand identical angles imply similarity, and use side ratios or separate drawings for unknown lengths and angles.
SS4_desc_short: Students enhance understanding of similar shapes by calculating scale factors, identifying similar shapes, and computing unknown lengths and angles. They solve problems involving similar triangles and visualise problems by drawing shapes with common vertices or angles separately. Discussion of problem-solving approaches is encouraged, integrating topics like Pythagoras' theorem or trigonometry.
SS5_desc_short: Students examine similar triangles, using proofs to confirm similarity. They review angle rules, especially with parallel lines, to identify equal angles. Correct geometric notation is stressed for labelling angles and lengths. Clear, precise explanations strengthen justifications. Similarity is confirmed if corresponding angles are equal or side lengths are proportional. Pythagoras' theorem application is encouraged in similar right-angled triangles.
SS6_desc_short: Students learn to distinguish between congruent (identical) and similar (proportional) shapes. They categorise various shape variations, considering corresponding lengths and angles in different orientations. This enhances understanding of overlapping concepts and unique characteristics of congruence and similarity.
SS7_desc_short: Students learn to identify congruent triangle properties, recalling conditions of congruence (SSS, ASA, SAS, RHS). Key features and examples are highlighted, including common misconceptions. They understand the minimum information needed for triangle congruence, distinguishing between similar and congruent triangles.

Small Step 1: Describe and continue arithmetic and geometric sequences
Small Step 3: Generate a sequence given an algebraic rule
Small Step 4: nth term of a linear sequence
SS1_desc: In this small step, students build on their knowledge of sequences from Key Stage 3 to describe and continue arithmetic and geometric sequences. Highlight to students that the terms “linear sequence” and “arithmetic sequence” are interchangeable. Remind students that an arithmetic sequence has a common difference whereas a geometric sequence has a common ratio. Students should have opportunities to use term-to-term rules to generate and describe sequences for both arithmetic and geometric sequences. Include questions where students find missing terms in sequences. Encourage students to look for patterns in a sequence to decide whether a number will appear later within it. It is not necessary to consider nth term rules in this step as this will be covered later in the block.
SS2_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding of sequences from Key Stage 3 to explore less familiar sequences, including Fibonacci sequences and triangular numbers, in greater depth. Encourage students to pattern spot to find rules for sequences. Sequences with algebraic terms can be explored, such as 2a, 3a, 5a, 8a. Term-to-term rules will be given in words, and it is not required to look at algebraic rules for sequences within this step. Concrete manipulatives, such as multi-link cubes, can allow students to represent sequences and support their reasoning.
SS3_desc: In this small step, students substitute values into expressions to generate sequences. The step focuses largely on arithmetic sequences, but some more complex types of sequence, such as quadratic and geometric, are introduced. Some students may need to recap substitution and order of operations prior to this step. Highlight to students that the value of n corresponds to the position of a term in the sequence. Substituting a value of n into an nth term rule gives the value of the corresponding term. Ensure students are confident in this as it will support the later step on deriving nth term rules of linear sequences. If appropriate, students can explore questions that require forming and solving equations to find the position of a particular value in a sequence.
SS4_desc: In this small step, students review finding the nth term rule for a linear sequence from Key Stage 3. Note that this may be the first time students have found the nth term of a sequence, as this is not covered in the Key Stage 3 Support curriculum. Make explicit that the terms “arithmetic sequence” and “linear sequence” are interchangeable. Begin by exploring increasing sequences before moving on to decreasing sequences. Students who are comfortable with integer sequences can be extended with sequences with fractional and decimal terms. Ensure that students also have opportunity to explore questions that require using the nth term rule to determine whether a number exists in a sequence.
SS5_desc: In this small step, students use algebra to represent types of numbers. Begin by guiding students to recognise that even numbers are all multiples of 2 and can be written as 2n, where n is an integer. Then encourage students to make connections between odd and even numbers, recognising that odd numbers are 1 more than even numbers and can be written as 2n + 1 Progress to exploring the results of performing the four operations on combinations of odd and even numbers, for example odd + odd = even. Using multilink cubes can support students to build and visualise number structures. Students will also be required to represent consecutive numbers algebraically, so it is vital they understand the concept of consecutive integers. Students should have the opportunity to discuss representations of multiples. For example, 6a (where a is an integer) being a multiple of 6 but also of 2 and 3
SS6_desc: In this small step, students use their knowledge of representing numbers algebraically to explore algebraic arguments and use counterexamples to disprove conjectures. It may be necessary to recap the terms prime number, square and cube. Begin by looking at counterexamples to disprove simple numerical conjectures such as “all multiples of 3 are odd” before progressing to algebraic conjectures such as “if x is an odd number, 8x will always be odd.” This foundation step does not require students to construct formal algebraic proofs.
SS1_desc_short: Students expand on Key Stage 3 sequence knowledge to explore and extend arithmetic and geometric sequences, understanding their common difference and ratio respectively. They apply term-to-term rules to generate sequences, identify missing terms, and predict future numbers by recognising patterns. Nth term rules are not included at this stage.
SS2_desc_short: Students expand on Key Stage 3 sequence knowledge, delving into Fibonacci sequences and triangular numbers. They're encouraged to spot patterns and find sequence rules. Sequences with algebraic terms like 2a, 3a, 5a, 8a are explored. Term-to-term rules are provided; algebraic rules aren't required. Multi-link cubes aid representation and reasoning.
SS3_desc_short: Students substitute values into expressions to create sequences, focusing on arithmetic but also introducing quadratic and geometric sequences. They should understand that 'n' represents a term's position, and substituting 'n' into an nth term rule gives that term's value. This supports deriving nth term rules and solving related equations.
SS4_desc_short: Students review finding the nth term rule for linear sequences, previously introduced in Key Stage 3. The terms "arithmetic sequence" and "linear sequence" are interchangeable. They explore increasing and decreasing sequences, extending to fractional and decimal sequences. They also practice using the nth term rule to identify sequence numbers.
SS5_desc_short: Students use algebra to understand number types. They learn even numbers are multiples of 2 (2n), and odd numbers are 1 more than even (2n + 1). They explore results of operations on odd and even numbers. They represent consecutive numbers algebraically, discuss multiples, and visualise number structures.
SS6_desc_short: Students apply algebraic representation knowledge to explore arguments and disprove conjectures using counterexamples. Key terms include prime number, square, cube, and counterexamples. They start with numerical conjectures like "all multiples of 3 are odd", then progress to algebraic ones like "if x is odd, 8x is odd".

Small Step 1: Area and circumference of a circle
Small Step 6: Area and perimeter of compound shapes with circles
SS1_desc: In this small step, students consolidate their prior learning from Year 10 on calculating the area and circumference of a circle. Remind students of key vocabulary related to circles such as radius, diameter, circumference and area, and how these relate to corresponding formulae for area and circumference. At this stage students’ focus should be on when each formula is appropriate to use. Provide practice involving a mixture of rounding answers to a specified degree of accuracy, for example, 3 significant figures and expressing answers in terms of pi (π). Challenge students to work out the radius or diameter of circles when given the area, making links to solving equations and rearranging formulae.
SS2_desc: In this small step, students broaden their understanding of the area of circles by exploring the area of fractional parts of circles, specifically split into sectors. Encourage students to calculate the area of a circle first, before focusing on what fraction of this area is needed. Discuss different strategies to work with fractions, for example, 1 5 of 50 can be calculated as 50 × 1 5 or 50 ÷ 5. Working with familiar fractions provides a solid foundation for upcoming extend steps on calculating arc lengths and sector area. This helps support that approaches such as multiplying the area of a circle by θ 360 are not always needed.
SS3_desc: In this extend step, students build upon their understanding of finding the area of fractional parts of circles. Emphasise the connection to the area of a full circle, highlighting that the fraction of a circle's area taken up by a sector corresponds to its fraction of a full 360° turn. This supports students in understanding why, in general, the area of a sector with angle θ is found by θ 360 × πr2 Include examples where answers are left in terms of π as well as in decimal form to reinforce flexibility in representation. Ensure there is opportunity for students to work from a given area of a sector to calculate either the angle subtended by the arc or the radius of the sector.
SS4_desc: In this small step, students extend their understanding of circumference by finding lengths of fractional parts of the circles, specifically sectors. This step reinforces basic fraction skills and introduces the concept of an arc, helping students see that the arc length represents a portion of the full circumference. Working with familiar fractions provides a solid foundation to prepare students to calculate arc lengths without using a formal approach, for example, θ 360 × πd. Model how to calculate arc lengths first, before solving problems involving the perimeter of a sector.
SS5_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding from the previous step to find the arc length and the perimeter of a sector. Ensure students are confident in calculating the circumference from a givin radius before calculating the arc length and perimeter of a sector. Draw students' attention to the size of the angle, θ, subtended by the arc length and how this informs what fraction of the whole circle is represented by the sector with the same radius. Emphasise that θ 360 × πd calculates only the arc length and not the perimeter of the sector. After practising arc length calculations, guide students to find the perimeter by adding the two radii to the arc length. Include examples with answers both in terms of π and decimal form to support flexibility in representation. If appropriate students could also discuss "minor" and "major" sectors and arcs.
SS6_desc: In this small step, students explore the area and perimeter of compound shapes, building on their knowledge of fractional parts of circles. Encourage students to identify familiar 2-D shapes within a compound shape. Include a mixture of examples where compound shapes are adjoined, as well as where compound shapes are formed by removing a shape from another shape. Where relevant and appropriate, discuss efficient methods for problems such as those that include two congruent semicircles. Students may discuss whether they can use the formula for a whole circle, rather than performing separate calculations for each.
SS7_desc: In this small step, students apply their understanding from previous steps in the block to solve contextual problems involving circles. Goal-free prompts are effective to encourage exploration and build confidence with problem solving. Practice should include problems involving percentages and fractions, for example, determining what percentage of a shape is shaded. For multi-step problems, breaking down steps and organising work into tables can help students solve systematically and efficiently. Ensure students have opportunities to give their answer approximately (as a decimal) or in exact form (in terms of π).
SS1_desc_short: Students review Year 10 concepts: calculating circle area and circumference, understanding related terms (radius, diameter, circumference, area), and using appropriate formulas. Practice includes rounding to specific accuracy, expressing answers in pi terms, and solving for radius or diameter from given area, linking to equation solving and formula rearranging.
SS2_desc_short: Students expand their understanding of circle areas by examining fractional parts, particularly sectors. They should first calculate a circle's area, then determine the required fraction. Discuss strategies for fraction calculations, like 50 x 1/5 or 50 ÷ 5. This foundation aids in future arc length and sector area calculations.
SS3_desc_short: Students enhance understanding of circle area fractions, linking sector area to its fraction of a full 360° turn. This clarifies why sector area with angle θ is θ 360 × πr2. Examples in π and decimal forms foster flexibility. Students calculate angle or radius from a given sector area.
SS4_desc_short: Students enhance their understanding of circumference by determining fractional parts of circles, specifically sectors. This step strengthens fraction skills and introduces arc concept, representing a portion of full circumference. Familiar fractions prepare students to calculate arc lengths informally. Demonstrates calculating arc lengths before tackling sector perimeter problems.
SS5_desc_short: Students build on prior knowledge to calculate arc length and sector perimeter. Confidence in calculating circumference from a given radius is essential. The angle θ informs the fraction of the circle represented by the sector. After mastering arc length calculations, students add two radii to find the perimeter. Examples include π and decimal forms. Discuss "minor" and "major" sectors/arcs if relevant.
SS6_desc_short: Students examine compound shapes' area and perimeter, building on fractional circle knowledge. They identify 2-D shapes within compound shapes, including examples where shapes are adjoined or formed by removing one from another. Discussions on efficient methods for problems involving congruent semicircles are encouraged.
SS7_desc_short: Students apply prior knowledge to solve circle-related problems, using goal-free prompts for exploration and confidence. Practice includes percentages and fractions, like determining shaded shape portions. Multi-step problems are broken down and organised in tables for systematic solving. Students can answer approximately (decimal) or exactly (π terms).

macro_topic: Ratio, proportion and rates of change
SS1_desc: In this small step, students will revisit problems involving ratio covered in Year 10. Provide opportunities for students to share amounts in a ratio when given either a total, a part, or a difference. Bar models can help students to identify key differences between problems and to establish a suitable strategy to solve them, rather than relying on a procedural method, such as dividing an amount by the sum of the parts in a given ratio. Students should also explore links between fractions and ratios. Note that “combine a set of ratios” and “link ratio and algebra” are both Extend steps in Year 10 and may not have been covered previously. Time could be given to each of these steps if appropriate.
SS2_desc: In this small step, students will solve financial problems, building on prior learning from Key Stage 3. Provide students opportunities to explore different strategies to compare items and cost, for example, scaling to compare the same amount of each item or working out the unit cost of each item. It is important to discuss when different strategies may be more efficient than others. Students should also be able to solve problems with common everyday life special offers, for example, “20% off’” or “3 for 2” offers. The use of a calculator is recommended for this step to place more emphasis on strategies to solve problems rather than mental arithmetic.
SS3_desc: In this small step, students draw and interpret conversion graphs to complete approximate conversions. Explain to students that if a conversion graph forms a straight line passing through the origin, it represents a direct proportion. Encourage students to draw a line segment to the graph when reading a value from the axes. Students can also use the learning from the previous step to work out values that are not in the range of the data represented on a graph. For example, if a graph shows that 5 cm ≈ 2 inches, then 50 cm ≈ 20 inches. Unfamiliar contexts, such as converting metric units to imperial units, are useful to encourage students to use a graph for their conversions.
SS4_desc: In this small step, students will revisit exchange rates, which were covered in Key Stage 3. Explain what an exchange rate implies, for example, £1 = €1.30 means every £1 is worth €1.30 Model strategies to highlight the directly proportional relationship between two values. For example, if the number of euros is found by multiplying the number of pounds by 1.30, then the number of pounds can be found by dividing the number of euros by 1.30 Double number lines can be a useful representation to support students. Ensure that students practise with a variety of currencies. Everyday life contexts are also recommended, for example, comparing prices of items in different countries.
SS5_desc: In this small step, students will use their understanding of direct proportion to form equations. Students will be introduced to the symbol ∝, which is used to denote when two variables are proportional. For example, y ∝ x means y is proportional to x, which implies direct proportion. Highlight that direct proportion equations are written in the form y = kx, where x and y are variables and k is the constant of proportionality. Encourage students to substitute known information into their formula to work out the constant of proportionality. They will then use this constant to work out different corresponding values of the variables. Students can also solve problems in context. For example, the cost, c, of pens is directly proportional to the number of pens, p.
SS6_desc: In this small step, students will build on their understanding from the previous step to solve direct proportion equations involving powers and roots. Highlight the differences between formulae of different forms, such as y = kx2 and y = k x , and ensure that students are familiar with vocabulary linked to powers and roots. For example, x2 means “the square of x” and x means “the square root of x. Students should be reminded that equations such as x2 = 25 have a positive and negative solution.
SS7_desc: In this small step, students explore the relationship between quantities that are inversely proportional. Students should understand that if two quantities are inversely proportional, then when one amount increases, the other amount decreases at the same rate. Students’ attention should be drawn to the fact that the product of the two quantities is constant as the quantities vary. It may be useful to begin with a scenario involving one person, such as 1 person takes 4 hours to paint a fence, before moving on to more challenging problems. Encourage students to think about whether a task will take more or less time with more people. Throughout the step, discuss any assumptions that have been made, such as assuming every person works at the same rate.
SS8_desc: In this small step, students will build on their understanding from the previous step to form and solve inverse proportion equations. Students will learn that “y is inversely proportional to x” is the same as “y is directly proportional to 1 x ”, which can be written as y ∝ 1 x . They will also learn that inverse proportion formulae are written in the form y k x = , where k is the constant of proportionality. Once students can create equations in this form, provide opportunities to practise with more complex formulae such as y = k x2 or y = k x 3 , highlighting key vocabulary such as “the square of” or “the root of”. If appropriate, students could solve a combination of problems using both direct and inverse proportion.
SS9_desc: In this small step, students will plot and interpret graphical representations of directly and inversely proportional relationships, which they may have covered in Year 9. Remind students that direct proportion graphs form a straight line from the origin. Highlight that inversely proportional relationships produce a non-linear graph where the horizontal and vertical axis are asymptotes. When working with direct proportion graphs, ensure students can use proportional reasoning to complete conversions outside of the range of values of the graph. Provide students with plenty of opportunities to identify whether a graph represents a directly proportional or an inversely proportional relationship.
SS1_desc_short: Students revisit ratio problems from Year 10, sharing amounts in a ratio given a total, part, or difference. Bar models help identify problem differences and establish solution strategies. Links between fractions and ratios are explored. Steps like "combine ratios" and "link ratio and algebra" may need extra time.
SS2_desc_short: Students will solve financial problems, building on Key Stage 3 knowledge. They'll explore strategies to compare item costs, including scaling and unit cost calculation. Discussion on strategy efficiency is encouraged. Problem-solving includes everyday special offers like "20% off" or "3 for 2". Calculator use is recommended.
SS3_desc_short: Students learn to draw and interpret conversion graphs for approximate conversions, understanding that a straight line through the origin indicates direct proportion. They extrapolate values beyond the graph's range, using contexts like metric to imperial conversions. Drawing line segments aids in reading values from the axes.
SS4_desc_short: Students revisit exchange rates from Key Stage 3, learning that £1 = €1.30 signifies each £1 is worth €1.30. They explore the proportional relationship between currencies, using multiplication and division. Double number lines aid understanding. Practice involves various currencies and real-life price comparisons in different countries.
SS5_desc_short: Students learn direct proportion, denoted by ∝, forming equations like y = kx, where k is the constant of proportionality. They substitute known data to find k, then use it to calculate variable values. This concept is applied to real-world problems, like cost proportionality to quantity.
SS6_desc_short: Students will expand on prior knowledge to solve direct proportion equations with powers and roots. Emphasize differences between formulae like y = kx2 and y = k x, and ensure familiarity with terms related to powers and roots. Remind students that equations like x2 = 25 have positive and negative solutions.
SS7_desc_short: Students learn about inversely proportional quantities: when one increases, the other decreases at a consistent rate. They explore this through scenarios like painting a fence, considering how task duration changes with more people. The focus is on understanding that the product of the quantities remains constant.
SS8_desc_short: Students will advance their understanding of inverse proportion equations, learning that "y is inversely proportional to x" equates to "y ∝ 1/x". They'll practice creating and solving equations like y=kx² or y=kx³, using key terms like "the square of" or "the root of".
SS9_desc_short: Students will plot and interpret graphs of direct and inverse proportions, previously covered in Year 9. They'll learn that direct proportions form a straight line from the origin, while inverse proportions create non-linear graphs with horizontal and vertical axis asymptotes. They'll practice identifying these relationships and using proportional reasoning.

Small Step 4: Surface area of spheres and cones
Small Step 5: Volume and surface area of a frustrum (E)
Small Step 6: Convert metric units of area and volume
SS1_desc: In this small step, students will draw accurate and scaled plans as well as front and side elevations of 3-D shapes. They should begin by drawing plans and elevations of cubes and cuboids before moving onto more complex shapes such as prisms. Allowing students access to physical objects such as interlocking cubes to make shapes or dynamic geometry software may be useful to help students visualise objects before attempting to draw plans and elevations. If appropriate, have students draw or describe shapes when given the plan and elevation views. This includes being able to draw them on isometric paper, which was covered in the previous step.
SS2_desc: In this small step, students use the formula to calculate the volume of a sphere. Tell students that the formula to work out the volume of a sphere with radius r is 4 3 πr3, and model how to input relevant calculations into a calculator, for example 4 3 × π × 53, to work out the volume of a sphere with a radius of 5 cm. As with other formulae involving pi (π), students should have the opportunity to provide exact answers in terms of pi (π), as well as giving approximate answers to varying degrees of accuracy. When students are comfortable with finding the volume of a sphere, they could begin to investigate the volume of hemispheres and compound shapes.
SS3_desc: In this small step, students calculate the volume of cones and pyramids. Students may not have covered this before, as this is an extend step in Year 9. When introducing the volume of a cone, it may be useful to draw out similarities with the formula for the volume of a cylinder. That is, the volume of a cone is 1 3 of the volume of a cylinder with the same height and radius. Then further similarity may be shown between the formulae for cones and pyramids. For example, the volume of both can be calculated with 1 3 × area of base × height. Provide opportunities for students to work backwards from a given volume to find dimensions of the cone or pyramid. If appropriate, explore problems where using Pythagoras’ theorem may be necessary to calculate the perpendicular height of the cone or pyramid.
SS4_desc: In this small step, students will use formulae to calculate the surface area of spheres and cones. The formulae for each of these are given, although it should be noted that the formula given for the surface area of a cone is often only for the curved surface area. As with previous steps, students should be encouraged to give answers to varied degrees of accuracy. They should also be given opportunities to rearrange formulae, such as calculating the radius of a cone when given the surface area and slant height. Once comfortable with spheres and cones, students can investigate the surface area of related shapes such as hemispheres or other 3-D solids made from these.
SS5_desc: In this small step, students work out volume and surface area of frustums made from cones or pyramids. Students may need reminding of strategies to work out unknown lengths in similar shapes, as this will often be needed to access frustum problems. It may also be useful to scaffold problems initially to build confidence, for example, working out the volume of a cone then “removing” a smaller and mathematically similar cone to create a frustum. Students should solve problems involving both volume and surface area, rounding answers to varied degrees of accuracy.
SS6_desc: In this small step, students convert metric units of area and volume. This may have been previously studied in the extend step in Year 9. Initially, explore the area of a square 1 cm in length and compare it to the area of a square 10 mm in length, to emphasise how 1 cm² is equal to 100 mm². A similar approach can be used to convert units of volume using a 1 cm cube compared to the volume of a 10 mm cube. Students should recognise that the area scale factor is the square of the linear scale factor, and the volume scale factor is the cube of the linear scale factor. Students can then progress to converting between units in a single calculation. Links between other measurements of volume such as m3 and cm3 should also be explored.
SS1_desc_short: Students will create scaled plans and elevations of 3-D shapes, starting with cubes and cuboids, then advancing to prisms. Using interlocking cubes or dynamic geometry software can aid visualisation. They may also draw or describe shapes from given plans and elevations, including on isometric paper.
SS2_desc_short: Students learn to calculate sphere volume using the formula 4/3 πr3, including inputting calculations into a calculator (e.g., 4/3 × π × 53 for a 5cm radius sphere). They should provide exact and approximate answers, and when comfortable, explore hemisphere and compound shape volumes.
SS3_desc_short: In this step, Year 9 students learn to calculate cone and pyramid volumes, drawing parallels with cylinder volume formulas. They explore the concept that cone and pyramid volumes are 1/3 of the base area times height. Students also practice reverse calculations and apply Pythagoras' theorem when needed.
SS4_desc_short: Students learn to use formulas to calculate the surface area of spheres and cones, including rearranging formulas for varied calculations like finding cone radius from surface area and slant height. Emphasis on accuracy and exploring surface areas of related 3D shapes like hemispheres is encouraged.
SS5_desc_short: Students calculate volume and surface area of frustums derived from cones or pyramids. They apply strategies to determine unknown lengths in similar shapes, often required for frustum problems. Initially, problems may be scaffolded, like finding a cone's volume before creating a frustum. Students solve problems involving volume and surface area, rounding answers accordingly.
SS6_desc_short: Students convert metric units of area and volume, comparing squares and cubes of different lengths to understand conversion. They learn that area scale factor is the square, and volume scale factor is the cube, of the linear scale factor. This progresses to single-calculation unit conversions and exploring m³ to cm³ conversions.

Small Step 1: Find unknown lengths and angles in similar shapes
Small Step 4: Prove a pair of triangles are similar
Small Step 7: Solve problems with area and volume in similar shapes
Small Step 8: Prove a pair of triangles are congruent
SS1_desc: In this small step, students calculate unknown angles and lengths in similar shapes, building on learning from Key Stage 3. Ensure students identify corresponding sides correctly, drawing attention to examples where the shapes have different orientations. Model strategies to calculate the scale factor of enlargement between the shapes, ensuring students can work with both integer and fractional scale factors, for example the scale factor from shape A to B and shape B to A. Students should be aware that corresponding angles in similar shapes are equal. Encourage the use of geometric notation when labelling side lengths and angles.
SS2_desc: In this small step, students apply their knowledge of angle facts to show that a pair of triangles are similar. Remind students of facts involving angles that are vertically opposite, angles that form a straight line and angles that exist within pairs of parallel lines. Ensure students see a variety of examples including triangles that share a vertex as well triangles embedded within each other, sharing a common angle. Support students to identify the corresponding vertices of two similar triangles. Highlight that if all three angles in one triangle are the same as the angles in another, then the triangles are similar. If necessary, it may be appropriate to encourage students to draw the triangles separately and in the same orientation before working out unknown lengths and angles. Students should also recognise that using side ratios is an equally valid method of establishing similarity.
SS3_desc: In this small step, students consolidate and extend their understanding of similar shapes. Begin with a mixture of questions from previous steps where students calculate scale factors between similar shapes, determining if two shapes are similar and calculating unknown lengths and angles. Once students are confident, include problems involving similar triangles. Demonstrate strategies to help visualise the problem, such as drawing any shapes with a common vertex or angle as two separate shapes in the same orientation. Students should be encouraged to discuss their approaches and reasoning when solving the problems. It may be appropriate to interleave other topic areas such as Pythagoras’ theorem or trigonometry.
SS4_desc: In this extend step, students explore general examples of similar triangles and use formal proofs to justify their similarity. They may need reminders about angle rules, such as those involving parallel lines, to help identify equal angles within a shape. Emphasise the importance of using correct geometric notation when labelling angles and lengths in their proofs. Demonstrate how to structure explanations clearly and precisely to strengthen their justifications. Reinforce that triangles are similar if all corresponding angles are equal or if their corresponding side lengths are proportional. Where appropriate, challenge students to apply Pythagoras’ theorem within similar right-angled triangles.
SS5_desc: In this small step, students explore the relationship between the areas of similar shapes. Start by guiding students to spot patterns between the scale factors for lengths and areas, highlighting that the area scale factor is the square of the length scale factor. Note that length scale factor is also known as the linear scale factor. Students should explore identifying area scale factors that are not integers, expressing such examples as a fraction instead of decimal. Once students are confident in identifying the area scale factor, extend their understanding to find unknown areas or lengths in 2-D shapes, including opportunities to practise working with surface areas of 3-D shapes. If appropriate, make the connection to learning from the previous block where students converted between metric units of area.
SS6_desc: In this small step, students explore the links between the volumes of similar 3-D shapes. Like the previous step, begin by calculating the volumes of similar cubes and cuboids and draw students’ attention to the relationship between the length scale factor and the volume scale factor. Students should understand the volume scale factor is the cube of the length scale factor. Ensure students are familiar with representing the volumes of similar solids as a ratio. Encourage non-integer scale factors to be expressed as fractions instead of decimals. Once students are confident with identifying the volume scale factor between two similar solids, have them use it to calculate unknown volumes and lengths of 3-D shapes. Calculating unknown volumes from given surface areas of similar solids is covered in the next step.
SS7_desc: In this small step, students consolidate their understanding of calculating areas and volumes in similar shapes from previous steps. Students will also apply their understanding of calculating surface area and volumes of more complex 3-D shapes from the previous step. Begin by recapping calculating area and volume scale factors before using this to find the areas and volumes of similar shapes. Students should also explore problems where the surface area or volume of both shapes is given. Encourage students to use ratios to calculate any unknown lengths, areas or volumes. Scaffolding multi-step problems can help students access these types of problems.
SS8_desc: In this small step, students will identify and establish the difference between congruent and similar shapes, using conditions of congruence. Remind them that congruent shapes are identical, while similar shapes are proportional to each other. It may be necessary to remind students of the conditions of congruence such as SSS, ASA, SAS and RHS. Begin by defining each condition of congruence, highlighting the key features and showing various examples and non-examples for each. Students should understand the minimum information needed to establish congruence. Ensure students see examples that are commonly mistaken as congruent but do not present enough information, for example, two triangles with three angles in common are similar, but more information would be needed to determine if the triangles are congruent.
SS1_desc_short: Students calculate unknown angles and lengths in similar shapes, building on Key Stage 3 knowledge. They identify corresponding sides, even in differently oriented shapes, and calculate the scale factor of enlargement, using both integer and fractional factors. They understand that corresponding angles in similar shapes are equal and use geometric notation.
SS2_desc_short: Students apply angle knowledge to identify similar triangles, using facts about vertically opposite, straight line, and parallel line angles. They examine various examples, including overlapping triangles, and identify corresponding vertices. Similarity is established through equal angles or side ratios. Drawing separate, similarly oriented triangles may aid understanding.
SS3_desc_short: Students enhance understanding of similar shapes by calculating scale factors, identifying similar shapes, and determining unknown lengths and angles. They solve problems involving similar triangles, visualise problems by drawing shapes separately, and discuss their problem-solving strategies. Interleaving topics like Pythagoras' theorem or trigonometry may be beneficial.
SS4_desc_short: Students examine similar triangles, using formal proofs and angle rules for justification. Emphasis is on correct geometric notation for angles and lengths, and clear, precise explanations. Similarity in triangles is defined by equal corresponding angles or proportional sides. Application of Pythagoras' theorem in similar right-angled triangles is encouraged.
SS5_desc_short: Students examine the link between areas of similar shapes, focusing on the relationship between length and area scale factors. They identify non-integer area scale factors, expressing them as fractions. This understanding is extended to finding unknown areas or lengths in 2-D and 3-D shapes, and converting metric units of area.
SS6_desc_short: Students explore the connection between volumes of similar 3D shapes. They calculate volumes of cubes and cuboids, understanding the volume scale factor as the cube of the length scale factor. They represent volumes as ratios, express non-integer scale factors as fractions, and use these to calculate unknown volumes and lengths.
SS7_desc_short: Students reinforce calculating areas and volumes of similar and complex 3-D shapes. They recap area and volume scale factors, apply these to find areas and volumes of similar shapes, and solve problems with given surface area or volume. They use ratios for unknown lengths, areas, or volumes, and scaffold multi-step problems.
SS8_desc_short: Students will distinguish between congruent and similar shapes, understanding that congruent shapes are identical and similar shapes are proportional. They'll learn conditions of congruence (SSS, ASA, SAS, RHS), identify key features, and recognize common misconceptions about congruence. Minimum information needed for establishing congruence will be emphasized.

Small Step 3: Describe and continue sequences involving surds (E)
Small Step 4: Generate a sequence given an algebraic rule
Small Step 5: nth term of a linear sequence
Small Step 6: nth term of a quadratic sequence (E)
SS1_desc: In this small step, students build on their knowledge of sequences from Key Stage 3 to explore arithmetic and geometric sequences, as well as less familiar sequences including quadratic and Fibonacci sequences, and triangular numbers. Allow students to become familiar with the definitions of “arithmetic”, “geometric” and “Fibonacci”. Highlight to students that the terms “linear sequence” and “arithmetic sequence” are interchangeable. Students should have opportunities to use term-to-term rules to generate and describe sequences. Include questions where students find missing terms in sequences. Encourage students to pattern spot to find rules for sequences. Sequences with algebraic terms may be introduced, but keep in mind that this is specifically looked at in the next step. It is not necessary to consider nth term rules in this step, as this is covered later in the block.
SS2_desc: In this small step, students explore arithmetic, geometric and Fibonacci sequences that have algebraic terms. Students will be expected to use their knowledge of common difference and common ratio and the structure of Fibonacci sequences within this step. Ensure students are confident in manipulating algebraic expressions, as this is essential for finding missing algebraic terms within sequences. Students may be required to form and solve simultaneous equations to find values of unknowns, or factorise expressions to find common ratios. This step does not involve students using or finding nth term rules.
SS3_desc: In this small step, students build on their knowledge from previous steps to explore sequences where the terms, common differences or common ratios are surds. It may be necessary to recap manipulating expressions involving surds before introducing this step to students. Students will be required to use the four operations with surds as well as expand double brackets involving surds. This step includes arithmetic, geometric and Fibonacci sequences. Highlight that simplifying expressions can help with recognising patterns in sequences, for example, in a geometric sequence with 1st and 2nd terms 3 3 and 6 12 , it is easier to see the common ratio is 4 when the 2nd term is simplified to 12 3 . It is not necessary for students to use or work out nth term rules in this step.
SS4_desc: In this small step, students substitute values into expressions to generate sequences. The step focuses on a variety of sequences, including arithmetic, quadratic and geometric. Some students may need to recap substitution and order of operations prior to this step. Highlight to students that the value of n corresponds to the position of a term in the sequence and that substituting a value of n into an nth term rule gives the value of the corresponding term. Ensure students are confident in this, as it will support the later step on the nth term rule. If appropriate, students can explore questions that require forming and solving equations or inequalities to find the position of a particular value in a sequence.
SS5_desc: In this small step, students will review finding the nth term rule for a linear sequence from Key Stage 3. Make explicit that the terms “arithmetic sequence” and “linear sequence” are interchangeable. Begin by exploring increasing sequences before moving on to decreasing sequences. Students who are comfortable with integer sequences can be extended with sequences containing fractional and decimal terms. Ensure that students also have opportunity to explore questions that require using the nth term rule to determine whether a number exists in a sequence.
SS6_desc: In this small step, students are introduced to finding the nth term of a quadratic sequence for the first time. Begin by looking at sequences of the form n2 + c. Focus students on the difference between these sequences and the sequence n2. Discuss the pattern of differences and second differences before introducing sequences of the form an2, drawing attention to the value of a being half the second difference. Once students are confident, move to finding nth terms of sequences of the form an2 + bn and finally sequences of the form an2 + bn + c. Problems that involve finding whether a number is a term of a quadratic sequence or finding the position of a particular term can be explored if appropriate. Students may need reminding of methods for solving quadratics prior to attempting these problems.
SS7_desc: In this small step, students use algebra to represent types of numbers. Begin by guiding students to recognise that even numbers are all multiples of 2 and can be written as 2n, where n is an integer. Then encourage students to make connections between odd and even numbers, recognising that odd numbers are 1 more than even numbers and can be written as 2n + 1 Progress to exploring the results of performing the four operations on combinations of odd and even numbers, for example odd + odd = even. Using multilink cubes can support students to build and visualise number structures. Students will also be required to represent consecutive numbers algebraically, so it is vital they understand the concept of consecutive integers. Students should have the opportunity to discuss representations of multiples. For example, 6a (where a is an integer) being a multiple of 6 but also of 2 and 3
SS8_desc: In this small step, students use their knowledge of representing numbers algebraically to complete algebraic proofs. Students will need to be comfortable with algebraic manipulation, so begin by recapping simplifying expressions and expanding and factorising quadratics. Draw attention to the need for rigorous algebraic proof rather than repeated numerical examples. Explore factorisations of expressions to highlight specific properties, for example, 12m + 6 can be shown to be a multiple of 2, 3 or 6 depending on the chosen factorisation. Students will require the knowledge that if n is an integer, n + 1 represents the next consecutive integer, and that 2n will be even and 2n + 1 will be odd.
SS1_desc_short: Students expand their Key Stage 3 sequence knowledge to explore arithmetic, geometric, quadratic, Fibonacci sequences, and triangular numbers. They learn that "linear sequence" equals "arithmetic sequence", generate sequences using term-to-term rules, find missing terms, and spot patterns. Sequences with algebraic terms may be introduced.
SS2_desc_short: Students explore arithmetic, geometric, and Fibonacci sequences with algebraic terms. They apply knowledge of common difference, common ratio, and Fibonacci structure. Mastery in manipulating algebraic expressions is crucial for identifying missing terms. They may form/solve simultaneous equations, factorise expressions, or find common ratios. No nth term rules involved.
SS3_desc_short: Students expand on prior knowledge to explore sequences with surds as terms, common differences, or ratios. They'll manipulate surd expressions, use four operations with surds, and expand double brackets. The step covers arithmetic, geometric, and Fibonacci sequences, emphasizing simplification for pattern recognition. Nth term rules aren't required.
SS4_desc_short: Students substitute values into expressions to create sequences, including arithmetic, quadratic, and geometric. They learn that 'n' represents a term's position in the sequence and substituting 'n' into an nth term rule yields the corresponding term's value. This prepares them for forming and solving equations or inequalities.
SS5_desc_short: Students review finding the nth term rule for linear (arithmetic) sequences from Key Stage 3, starting with increasing, then decreasing sequences. Those proficient with integer sequences can explore fractional and decimal sequences. Students should also apply the nth term rule to ascertain if a number exists in a sequence.
SS6_desc_short: Students learn to find the nth term of a quadratic sequence, starting with n2 + c sequences. They compare these to n2 sequences, examine patterns and differences, then progress to an2, an2 + bn, and an2 + bn + c sequences. Methods for solving quadratics and identifying sequence terms are also covered.
SS7_desc_short: Students use algebra to identify even (2n) and odd (2n + 1) numbers, exploring results of operations on them (e.g., odd + odd = even). They represent consecutive numbers algebraically, understanding consecutive integers. They discuss representations of multiples, like 6a being a multiple of 2, 3, and 6.
SS8_desc_short: Students apply knowledge of algebraic representation to complete proofs. They should be proficient in simplifying expressions, expanding and factorising quadratics. Emphasise rigorous algebraic proof over numerical examples. Explore expression factorisations to demonstrate properties. Understand n as an integer, n+1 as the next integer, and 2n and 2n+1 as even and odd.

Small Step 2: Circle theorem (angles at the centre and circumference)
Small Step 4: Circle theorem (angles in the same segment)
Small Step 5: Circle theorem (angles in a cyclic quadrilateral)
Small Step 6: Circle theorem (perpendicular from the centre to a chord)
Small Step 7: Circle theorem (angle between radius and tangent)
Small Step 8: Circle theorem (two tangents from a point)
SS1_desc: In this small step, students focus on finding angles in circles, specifically using properties of isosceles triangles and angles on a straight line. Remind students how to work out unknown angles in isosceles triangles and on straight lines and ensure they can interpret angle notation. Model how to annotate diagrams to identify isosceles triangles, for example, marking the radii with hatch marks to indicate they are equal in length. Encourage students to look for instances where angles form a straight line, allowing them to form an equation equal to 180° to find the value of unknown angles. As students progress, introduce more complex circle diagrams that combine the angle properties of isosceles triangles and straight lines, helping them develop a multi-step approach to solving geometry problems.
SS2_desc: In this small step, students begin learning the first of eight circle theorems. They explore how the angle at the centre of a circle subtended by an arc is twice the angle at the circumference subtended by the same arc. Students draw upon their prior knowledge of various angle facts from Key Stage 3, such as the sum of the interior angles of a triangle. Dynamic geometry software is an effective visual tool to investigate the relationship between the angle at the centre and the angle at the circumference, as it allows students to see how these angles change with different arrangements. It is also beneficial to include examples with chords and angles in various orientations, not just when the angle at the circumference is at the “top” of the circle.
SS3_desc: In this small step, students explore the theorem exploring how any angle inscribed in a semicircle (subtended by the diameter) is always 90°. Using dynamic geometry software can help students to see this is the case no matter where on the circumference the vertex of the angle is. Encourage students to apply their understanding of angles in a triangle to solve problems with triangles inscribed in a semicircle. They should annotate diagrams by labelling the right angle and discuss strategies to work out unknown angles. Additionally, explain that this theorem can be seen as a special case of the previous step, where the angle at the centre of the circle is 180°. If appropriate, previous topics involving right-angled triangles, such as Pythagoras’ theorem and trigonometry, can be included in circle problems for students to solve.
SS4_desc: In this small step, students explore the theorem that states that angles in the same segment of a circle are equal. This theorem can be proven by using the theorem that states the angle at the centre of a circle is twice the angle at the circumference. Ensure students can identify major and minor segments in a circle. Drawing an auxiliary chord can support students to see that the angles are in the same segment. The use of dynamic geometry software is a useful visual to show students a variety of diagrams to help them recognise the relationship beyond the typical “bow-tie” shape. As they deepen their understanding, students can combine this theorem with the circle theorems covered earlier in the block to tackle more complex problems. Encourage students to justify each step of their working by explaining their reasoning clearly.
SS5_desc: In this small step, students explore the theorem that opposite angles in a cyclic quadrilateral sum to 180°. Ensure students have a secure knowledge of the properties of different quadrilaterals and the sum of their interior angles. Emphasise that a quadrilateral is cyclic when all four vertices lie on the circumference of a circle. Encourage students to measure pairs of opposite angles and observe that they always add up to 180°. The use of dynamic geometry software to manipulate the shape can help reinforce this concept, showing that the relationship remains consistent regardless of where the vertices lie on the circumference. This relationship can also be proven by connecting it to the theorem that the angle at the centre is twice the angle at the circumference. As students’ understanding develops, encourage students to solve problems involving other circle theorems covered so far in the block.
SS6_desc: In this small step, students explore the theorem that a perpendicular line segment from the centre of a circle to a chord, bisects the chord. Ensure students are confident in their understanding of what a chord is. Emphasise the fact that when a radius bisects a chord, the angle between the radius and the chord is always 90°. The use of dynamic geometry software allows students to manipulate the position of the chord and to observe that the perpendicular relationship remains consistent. If appropriate, relate this topic to construction and loci, modelling that a perpendicular bisector of a chord must pass through the centre of a circle, and doing this twice on separate chords in turn locates the centre of the circle.
SS7_desc: In this small step, students explore the theorem that the angle between a radius and tangent of a circle, that meet at the same point on the circumference, is 90°. Ensure students understand that a tangent is a line that touches the circumference of the circle at only one point.
