They need to understand that vertically opposite angles are formed when two straight lines cross, and if either of the lines are not straight, then the angles formed are not vertically opposite. Secure this understanding by comparing vertically opposite angles to pairs of angles around a point that are opposite each other, but not vertically opposite, i.e. they are not formed by two straight lines intersecting. Once children understand that vertically opposite angles are equal, they can use this fact alongside the rules they already know to work out missing angles.
SS4_desc: learn that the interior angles of a triangle always sum to 180°. Get children to measure each angle of a triangle and add them together, doing this for a number of different triangles, in order to discover the rule. Discuss that, when using a protractor, there is the possibility of small inaccuracies causing the total to be slightly different from 180°. When the rule is established, children work out unknown angles in triangles. They should see each angle as a “part” and 180° as the “whole”. The three parts add to make the whole. This means that they can work out one of the missing parts by subtracting each of the known parts from the whole, or adding the known parts together before subtracting this from the whole. This step is a good opportunity to revisit mental and written calculation methods, as well as using inverse operations to check answers.
SS5_desc: In Year 4, children learnt to classify triangles as equilateral, isosceles or scalene, based on the lengths of their sides. They also know that a right-angled triangle has one angle of 90°. extend this learning to include the angles of triangles. Using their knowledge of angles in specific triangles, as well as the total of the angles, children work out missing angles in different types of triangles. Starting with equilateral triangles, as all the angles are equal, children learn that each angle must be 180° ÷ 3 = 60°. They then move on to investigating isosceles triangles. Children learn that not only do isosceles triangles have two equal sides, but they also have two equal angles. They need to identify which two angles are equal in order to find the sizes of unknown angles in the triangles. Children may need reminding of hatch mark notation to show that sides of shapes are equal in length.
SS6_desc: combine what they have learnt so far in this block to solve a variety of missing angle questions. By thinking about angles in different types of triangles, as well as in right angles, on a straight line and around a point, children should be able to work out the sizes of missing angles in increasingly complex problems. Begin by recapping the rules of angles they have learnt so far, and then share a problem with the class and discuss what methods are available based on the facts they know. Work through missing angle problems that begin with one focus, but move on to examples that require knowledge of more than one rule. At each stage, ask children to explain what rules they have used to solve the problem. They will find that there are multiple ways of solving most problems, and this will consolidate their understanding of the rules.
SS7_desc: In Year 4, children explored the properties of different quadrilaterals and they should be familiar with the words “trapezium”, “rhombus”, “square”, “rectangle”, “parallelogram” and “kite”. They learnt about the equal and parallel sides in quadrilaterals, as well as which ones have right angles. In this small step, that learning is extended to include the properties of the angles in these quadrilaterals. For a square and a rectangle, the fact that the angles add up to 360° can be worked out quickly. For other quadrilaterals, children can investigate by measuring the angles with a protractor. Show that, as any quadrilateral can be split into two triangles, the sum of the interior angles is twice that of a triangle and compare this with the totals found by measuring. Children then move on to explore the relationships between angles in a rhombus and a parallelogram, where opposite angles are equal.
SS8_desc: develop their understanding of interior angles in 2-D shapes by looking at polygons with five or more sides. Building on the fact that a quadrilateral can be split into two triangles, so the interior angles add up to 180 × 2 = 360°, children explore how many triangles polygons with a greater number of sides can be split into using a vertex of the polygon. They learn that the number of triangles is two fewer than the number of sides. Multiplying the number of triangles by 180° gives the sum of the interior angles in the polygon. Using this information, they can find unknown angles for any polygon. The main focus of this step is on regular polygons, where children can divide the total by the number of sides to work out the size of each angle.
SS9_desc: Children used circles in pie charts in the Statistics block in the Spring term. In this small step, they develop their learning to ensure understanding of the words “radius”, “diameter” and “circumference”. Children need to understand the importance of the centre of a circle: it is the point that is an equal distance from every part of the edge of the circle. They then move on to looking at the connection between the radius and the diameter. It is important that they realise that both of these are related to the centre. Showing examples and non-examples of radii and diameters will help to reinforce this understanding. At this stage, children do not need to be able to calculate the circumference. It may be useful to discuss that using a piece of string around the outside of a circle, then measuring it, will give an approximate measure for this.
SS10_desc: use skills learnt so far in this block to accurately draw shapes when given specific dimensions. Children begin drawing simple shapes that can be done on squared paper, such as rectangles and right-angled triangles where the base and height are given. This could be extended to drawing shapes where the perimeter and some of the sides are known. Children then produce an accurate drawing of a shape with known angles. They may need to begin by practising using a protractor. Children should work systematically, starting with a side and then drawing the angle(s) from the correct end(s). They should also use their understanding of the features of different quadrilaterals and triangles to recreate these accurately. This step is a good opportunity to revisit converting between centimetres and millimetres.
SS11_desc: In the final small step of this block, children learn that they can make a 3-D shape using knowledge of the 2-D shapes that make up its faces. Children should be familiar with 3-D shapes from earlier years, but you may need to remind them how to describe these shapes using edges, faces, vertices and curved surfaces. They should explore this step practically, starting with nets of a cube, made up of six squares, and investigating which arrangements will and will not fold to make a cube. Children can then move on to looking at other 3-D shapes and what 2-D shapes are needed to make their nets. Again, they first need to explore this with cut-out nets, which will help them to become more adept at visualising how nets fold up. Children can then work from a 3-D shape to decide how the net will look.
SS1_desc_short: In Year 5, students expand on Year 4's angle classifications (acute, right, obtuse) by including degrees and reflex angles. They practice estimating, classifying, and measuring angles using a protractor, ensuring correct scale use. This involves comparing angles to known fractions of a turn and understanding angle size in degrees.
SS2_desc_short: In Year 5, students revisit calculating missing angles on a straight line (180°) and around a point (360°). They learn the right angle symbol signifies 90° and without it, angle size is uncertain. They practice finding missing angles within a right angle and explore subtraction methods for angle calculations.
SS3_desc_short: Learn about vertically opposite angles, formed by intersecting straight lines, and their equality. Understand the difference between these and other opposite angles not formed by intersection. Use this knowledge, along with existing angle rules, to calculate missing angles.
SS4_desc_short: Learn that a triangle's interior angles sum to 180°. Measure and add angles from various triangles to understand this rule. Use a protractor, acknowledging possible inaccuracies. Once established, calculate unknown angles by viewing each as a part of the 180° whole. Revisit mental calculations and inverse operations for checking.
SS5_desc_short: In Year 4, students classify triangles (equilateral, isosceles, scalene) by side lengths and angles, including right-angles. They calculate missing angles using total triangle angles and specific triangle properties. They explore equilateral triangles' equal angles (180° ÷ 3 = 60°) and isosceles triangles' two equal sides and angles.
SS6_desc_short: Apply knowledge of angles in various triangles, right angles, straight lines, and points to solve complex missing angle problems. Recap angle rules, discuss problem-solving methods, and work through examples requiring multiple rules. Encourage students to explain their solutions, reinforcing their understanding of the rules.
SS7_desc_short: In Year 4, students learn properties of quadrilaterals like trapezium, rhombus, square, rectangle, parallelogram, and kite, including equal, parallel sides and right angles. They extend this to angle properties, using a protractor to measure and compare angles, and explore relationships between angles in a rhombus and parallelogram.
SS8_desc_short: Explore interior angles in 2-D polygons, particularly regular ones, by dividing them into triangles from a vertex. Understand that a polygon's triangles are two less than its sides, and multiplying this by 180° gives the total interior angles. Use this to calculate unknown angles and individual angle sizes.
SS9_desc_short: In this step, children learn about circles in pie charts, focusing on "radius", "diameter", and "circumference". They explore the circle's centre, equal distance from all edges, and the relationship between radius and diameter. Examples reinforce understanding. Circumference calculation isn't required, but approximation methods may be discussed.
SS10_desc_short: Children apply skills to draw shapes with given dimensions, starting with simple shapes on squared paper. They progress to shapes with known perimeters, sides, and angles, using a protractor as needed. They systematically draw sides and angles, using knowledge of quadrilaterals and triangles. Conversion between centimetres and millimetres is revisited.
SS11_desc_short: In this step, students learn to create 3-D shapes using 2-D shapes. They revisit descriptions of 3-D shapes using edges, faces, vertices, and curved surfaces. Practical exploration begins with cube nets, studying foldable arrangements. They then examine other 3-D shapes and their corresponding 2-D nets.

SS1_desc: This small step is designed to encourage students to talk mathematically and develop their confidence in contributing to class discussion. Students recognise and describe the change(s) from one term of a sequence to another. Ensure that they are exposed to both increasing and decreasing sequences and different types of sequences, such as linear, non-linear and oscillating. Students, however, are not expected to use these words to describe the sequences yet. Encourage students to make the sequences using resources such as cubes or counters to help them describe and continue sequences.
SS2_desc: In this small step, students identify changes in pictorial sequences and use these to work out subsequent terms. Students count the number of objects in each term of a pictorial sequence to generate a numerical sequence. Discuss the differences between terms, asking students to predict how many objects there will be in subsequent terms. Introduce them to sequences with more than one type of object in each term, for example 1 square and 4 circles, followed by 2 squares and 6 circles, and so on, where the number of squares represents the position of each term in the sequence. Ensure that students are exposed to both linear and non-linear sequences.
SS3_desc: In this small step, students recognise the difference between linear (also known as arithmetic) and non-linear sequences and use these words to describe them. Students learn that a linear sequence has a constant difference between terms and a non-linear sequence does not. Examples of non-linear sequences include geometric, quadratic and Fibonacci sequences. Although representing sequences graphically is not essential at this stage, appropriate technology/software could be used to highlight patterns and develop a deeper understanding of the terms “linear” and “non-linear”.
SS4_desc: In this small step, students develop their understanding from earlier in the block to continue sequences. The focus is solely on linear sequences, both ascending and descending, including decimals where appropriate. Model how to find the common difference between terms and apply this to work out subsequent terms in a given sequence. Students could also generate linear sequences from given information, for example the first term and the common difference. Calculators can be provided to offer further support and to help students develop their calculator skills.
SS5_desc: In this small step, students explore non-linear sequences. As with previous steps, encourage students to identify differences between terms to decide if the sequence is linear or non-linear. Introduce geometric sequences by providing some terms and a rule, for example double the previous term. Model strategies to work out the multiplier in a geometric sequence, such as dividing the 2nd term by the 1st term. Ensure that both increasing and decreasing sequences are used throughout. Students should also be given strategies to generate terms for other non-linear sequences, such as quadratic sequences. The use of calculators is recommended for this step to reduce cognitive load.
SS6_desc: In this small step, students find and use term-to-term rules. This step could be taught alongside the previous two steps. Encourage students to use full sentences and mathematical vocabulary to describe the term-to-term rule for any given sequence. Remind them that linear sequences have a constant difference and ensure that they describe rules with enough detail to avoid ambiguity, for example add 7 to the previous term. Model strategies to work out term-to-term rules for both linear and non-linear sequences, including Fibonacci sequences. The use of calculators is encouraged to reduce cognitive load.
SS7_desc: In this small step, students use a range of strategies to find missing terms in sequences where the rule cannot be determined from adjacent terms. Start with finding terms that are further away than the next term in a sequence. For example, finding the 6th term given the first three terms of a sequence. Provide examples where students are given the 1st term and another term (not the 2nd term) of a linear sequence, encouraging them to think about the number of common differences between the terms. As confidence develops, students can explore missing terms in both linear and non-linear sequences.
SS1_desc_short: This step boosts students' mathematical discussion skills. It focuses on recognising and describing changes in sequence terms, including increasing, decreasing, linear, non-linear, and oscillating sequences. Students are encouraged to use resources like cubes or counters to illustrate and extend sequences, enhancing their understanding.
SS2_desc_short: Students identify changes in pictorial sequences to predict subsequent terms. They count objects in each sequence term, generating a numerical sequence. Discussion includes differences between terms and sequences with multiple object types, where object count represents term position. Exposure to both linear and non-linear sequences is ensured.
SS3_desc_short: Students identify and describe linear (arithmetic) and non-linear sequences, understanding that linear sequences have a constant difference between terms, unlike non-linear ones. Non-linear sequences include geometric, quadratic, and Fibonacci sequences. Optional use of technology can highlight patterns, enhancing understanding of "linear" and "non-linear".
SS4_desc_short: Students enhance their understanding of linear sequences, both ascending and descending, including decimals. They learn to find the common difference between terms and use it to determine subsequent terms. They may also generate sequences from given information like the first term and common difference. Calculator use is encouraged.
SS5_desc_short: Students learn non-linear sequences, distinguishing them from linear ones. They explore geometric sequences, using strategies like dividing the 2nd term by the 1st to find multipliers. They practice with increasing, decreasing, and quadratic sequences, generating terms. Calculator use is advised to ease cognitive load.
SS6_desc_short: Students learn to find and use term-to-term rules in sequences, using mathematical vocabulary for clarity. They'll understand that linear sequences have a constant difference and apply detailed rules, such as adding 7 to the previous term. Strategies for linear, non-linear, and Fibonacci sequences are modeled, with calculator use encouraged.
SS7_desc_short: Students employ various strategies to identify missing sequence terms when the rule isn't clear from adjacent terms. This includes finding distant terms and understanding common differences between non-adjacent terms in a linear sequence. As proficiency grows, exploration of missing terms in linear and non-linear sequences is encouraged.

SS1_desc: In this small step, students become fluent in the use of 1-step function machines with numbers, working from both left to right and right to left. Before finding an input from a given output, check students’ understanding of inverse operations for the four operations, as well as for more complex functions such as squaring and square rooting. Encourage the use of calculators to help students to focus on the processes. Ensure that they are confident with using a calculator for functions such as square and square root.
SS2_desc: In this small step, students use their knowledge of function machines from the previous step to develop an understanding of algebraic notation. Concrete resources such as cubes and counters can be used to represent variables. Avoid using resources that have a predefined value, for example base 10, as these can lead to confusion around the definition of a variable. Discuss the conventional notation for algebra, for example 4a rather than a4 or 4 × a.
SS3_desc: In this small step, students identify the function that has taken place, given the input and the output of a function machine. It may be useful to begin by looking at examples with just numerical inputs and outputs, before moving on to algebraic ones. As in the previous step, using cubes and counters can help students to see the difference between multiplying a variable by a number or adding a number to a variable.
SS4_desc: In this small step, students substitute values into expressions, further developing their understanding of algebraic notation. Building on the previous steps, function machines can be used in conjunction with the step if appropriate. Check that students are confident with the meaning of algebraic notation such as 2x, x2 and x 2. This step provides an opportunity for students to develop calculator skills.
SS5_desc: In this small step, students move on to using two functions in a row, so that the output of the first function is the input of the second function. In this step, they become fluent with 2-step function machines with numbers, working both forwards and backwards. Begin by checking that students are confident using inverse operations with 1-step function machines to find the input from a given output. Encourage the use of calculators throughout if the arithmetic is a barrier.
SS6_desc: Building on the previous small step, students now explore 2-step function machines with algebra. Students may need a reminder of formal algebraic notation, for example y 5 for y ÷ 5. They need to understand that the order in which the functions are applied is important. They are introduced to brackets in algebraic expressions, to distinguish between, for example, 2x + 5 and 2(x + 5). Formal expansion of brackets is not expected at this stage. As in previous steps, cubes and counters can be used to support students’ understanding.
SS7_desc: In this small step, students develop their understanding of 2-step expressions by reversing the process of the previous step and finding the operations that formed the expressions. Students need to recognise the order in which the operations were performed to create the expression in the output, for example recognising the difference between 6(x + 2) and 6x + 2. Reinforce that the variable represents any number.
SS8_desc: In this small step, students substitute values into 2-step expressions, building on the skills they acquired earlier in the block. Discuss what is the same and what is different about expressions such as 3a + 2 and 3(a + 2) to support students’ understanding. Initially, students may find it useful to construct the expression as a function machine before inputting the value. As their confidence develops, they can progress to working more abstractly. This step also allows students to practise their calculator skills, and the use of calculators is encouraged throughout.
SS1_desc_short: Students learn to use 1-step function machines with numbers, operating in both directions. They should understand inverse operations for basic and complex functions like squaring and square rooting. Use of calculators is encouraged to focus on the process. Ensure confidence in using calculators for square and square root functions.
SS2_desc_short: Students apply prior knowledge of function machines to understand algebraic notation. Concrete resources like cubes and counters represent variables, avoiding items with set values to prevent confusion. The conventional algebraic notation, such as 4a, is discussed, promoting clarity in variable definition.
SS3_desc_short: Students identify operations in a function machine, starting with numerical inputs/outputs before progressing to algebraic ones. Using cubes and counters, they distinguish between multiplying a variable by a number and adding a number to a variable. This step builds on previous knowledge of number bonds, partitioning, and ten frames.
SS4_desc_short: Students enhance their algebraic notation understanding by substituting values into expressions. Function machines may assist. Ensure students grasp meanings of notations like 2x, x2, and x 2. This step also fosters calculator skills development.
SS5_desc_short: Students progress to using two sequential functions, where the first function's output is the second's input. They practice with 2-step function machines, operating forwards and backwards. Ensure students can use inverse operations with 1-step machines to determine inputs from outputs. Encourage calculator use if arithmetic is challenging.
SS6_desc_short: Advancing from prior lessons, students examine 2-step algebraic function machines, requiring understanding of formal notation like y ÷ 5, and the significance of function order. They learn about brackets in algebraic expressions, differentiating between 2x + 5 and 2(x + 5). Cubes and counters aid in comprehension.
SS7_desc_short: Students enhance their understanding of 2-step expressions by identifying the operations used to form them. They must discern the sequence of operations, distinguishing between expressions like 6(x + 2) and 6x + 2. It's crucial to remember that the variable can represent any number.
SS8_desc_short: Students substitute values into 2-step expressions, comparing expressions like 3a + 2 and 3(a + 2). They may initially use a function machine to construct expressions, progressing to abstract work as confidence grows. This step also enhances calculator skills, with calculator use encouraged.

SS1_desc: In this small step, students begin to understand the meaning of equality and equivalence. Students often misinterpret the equals sign as meaning “makes”. This could become a barrier with equations such as 2 + 3 = 1 + 4. Emphasise the bidirectional nature of equality, so that students understand that the left side and right side of an equation are worth the same amount. It is helpful to read the equals sign as “is equal to” to support this. Although collecting like terms is covered later in this block, it is important to highlight the differences between symbols used, so students should also be given opportunities to explore identities such as 3m + 2m 5m. Encourage them to substitute different values into expressions to decide if they are equivalent. Calculators may be used.
SS2_desc: In this small step, students look at calculations and expressions, using them to write related facts. They may be familiar with fact families from previous key stages, for example if 3 + 2 = 5, then 2 + 3 = 5, 5 − 2 = 3 and 5 − 3 = 2. This step extends their knowledge of fact families in preparation for solving equations and recognising equivalent forms of the same equation. Discuss commutative and non-commutative relationships and highlight the idea of grouping versus sharing when using division. Bar models are a useful representation to highlight related facts. These can be provided and/or students can be encouraged to draw bar models from calculations or equations. They may need support drawing/interpreting bar models representing a subtraction or a division.
SS3_desc: In this small step, students identify like and unlike terms, which is vital in supporting simplification of algebraic expressions. Students need to understand that like terms have the same variables, such as 5m and 9m, but terms with different variables, such as 3x and 3y, are unlike. Include terms with negative and fractional coefficients, so that students appreciate that, for example, 5p and −3p are like terms. Algebra tiles can be useful to highlight the difference between linear and squared terms, for example identifying that x and x2 are unlike terms. Discuss commutativity when comparing terms with more than one variable, for example 3ab and 8ba are like terms as ab ba.
SS4_desc: In this small step, students apply the ideas of equivalence to algebraic expressions. Model simplifying expressions such as 3f + 4f by highlighting the coefficient of each term and adding these to work out the coefficient of the simplified term. Include examples of terms with a coefficient of 1 and discuss the convention of writing c rather than 1c. Work towards simplifying expressions with more than two terms and terms with squared or multiple variables, for example t2 or 4ab, and discuss the commutative properties of like terms such as 3pq and 3qp. Encourage students to use the symbol to indicate that expressions are identical but represented in a different way. Students should also be encouraged to identify expressions that can/cannot be simplified.
SS5_desc: In this small step, students solve 1-step equations involving addition or subtraction. Encourage students to use calculators where appropriate. Equations such as m + 5 = 9 can be useful to introduce the concept, but avoid using these too often, as students are likely to “spot” the answer rather than use inverse operations. Refer to letters as “unknowns” rather than “variables”, as for these types of equations there is only one possible solution. Ensure that students see a variety of equations, including where the unknown is on the right-hand side of the equation, as well as variety in the order of terms, for example 293 = 35 + 3x. Bar models may be a useful representation, building on students’ understanding from previous steps. Links to function machines from the previous block could also be made.
SS6_desc: In this small step, students solve 1-step equations involving multiplication or division. As with the previous step, calculators can be used to reduce cognitive load when finding solutions to these equations. This will allow for a variety of questions, helping to avoid misconceptions such as solutions always being integers. Present equations with the expressions on either side of the equation. Ensure that students have the opportunity to practise 1-step equations, interchanging between multiplication and division in order to encourage them to think about which operation they need to use. If appropriate, students can explore strategies to solve equations in the form 8 x = 5
SS7_desc: In this small step, students build on previous learning to solve 2-step equations. As with the previous steps, emphasise the use of inverse operations to solve equations. Although “spotting” the answer may allow students to write the correct solution, this method will become gradually more difficult as equations become more complex throughout Key Stages 3 and 4 Ensure that students practise solving equations in various forms, involving both multiplication and division, rather than just those always in the form ax + b = c. Cups and counters, bar models and function machines are useful representations to highlight key concepts. Use these alongside written abstract calculations.
SS1_desc_short: Students learn the concept of equality and equivalence, understanding that both sides of an equation hold the same value. They explore different mathematical symbols and identities, like "3m + 2m = 5m", and test equivalence by substituting values into expressions. The use of calculators is allowed.
SS2_desc_short: Students explore calculations and expressions to write related facts, extending their understanding of fact families for equation solving. They discuss commutative and non-commutative relationships, and division's grouping versus sharing concept. Bar models are used to illustrate related facts, with guidance provided for subtraction or division representations.
SS3_desc_short: Students identify like and unlike terms to simplify algebraic expressions. Like terms share variables (5m, 9m), unlike terms don't (3x, 3y). Terms with negative/fractional coefficients (5p, -3p) and multiple variables (3ab, 8ba) are considered. Algebra tiles differentiate linear and squared terms (x, x2). Discusses commutativity.
SS4_desc_short: Students learn to simplify algebraic expressions, focusing on coefficients and terms with 1 coefficient or multiple variables. They explore commutative properties of like terms and use symbols to show identical expressions in different forms. The goal is to identify expressions that can or cannot be simplified.
SS5_desc_short: Students solve 1-step addition or subtraction equations, using calculators if needed. Equations like m + 5 = 9 introduce the concept, but variety is key to avoid answer-spotting and promote inverse operations. Terms like "unknowns" are used, and equations may position the unknown on either side.
SS6_desc_short: Students solve 1-step multiplication or division equations, using calculators to ease problem-solving. Questions vary to dispel misconceptions, like solutions always being integers. Equations present expressions on both sides. Practice interchanges between multiplication and division, promoting operation selection thought. If suitable, explore strategies for equations like 8 x = 5.
SS7_desc_short: Students build on prior knowledge to solve 2-step equations, using inverse operations. Discourage "spotting" answers as equations increase in complexity. Practice is needed in solving various forms of equations, involving multiplication and division. Use cups, counters, bar models, and function machines to illustrate key concepts alongside written calculations.

Small Step 1: Write integers in numerals and words
Small Step 11: Numbers greater than 1 in standard form E
Small Step 13: Numbers between 0 and 1 in standard form E
SS1_desc: In this small step, students practise reading and writing integers. Students have met numbers up to ten million at Key Stage 2, where they used commas as separators, but this scheme uses the same convention as GCSE, with no separators in numbers up to 9999 and spaces in numbers from 10 000 Start by checking students’ understanding of integer place value. Place value charts and part-whole models are useful for supporting students. Encourage them to focus on the structure of an integer, for example saying 5027 as “five thousand and twenty-seven” rather than just “five-zero-two-seven”. It is useful to apply a context to this step, such as population or government finances.
SS2_desc: In this small step, students work out intervals and position integers on number lines. Start with number lines where the start and end values are given, before exploring number lines where other values are given. Once students can demonstrate how to calculate intervals, they can start to use these to read values and position integers. Use number lines with a varying number of intervals and unmarked values in different positions. Making links to reading from common scales such as rulers, weighing scales and measuring jugs can be helpful. Students can then progress to estimating the position of an integer where fewer marks are given. Encourage them to find the midpoints of marked intervals to help them estimate. If required, students can use calculators for support.
SS3_desc: In this small step, students compare and order integers up to one billion. Students should use inequality symbols and precise mathematical language such as “greater than” and “less than”. Encourage them to read statements such as “1067 < 1607” from both left to right and right to left. They should be familiar with the equals symbol, but may need to be introduced to the symbol for “not equal” ( ). Students can then use their skills of identifying the values of digits in a number, supported by number lines if necessary, to put a series of integers in ascending or descending order. Emphasise the difference between a number and a digit, as students can get confused. Using numbers in different contexts, such as masses, wages and costs, may be useful.
SS4_desc: In this small step, students identify the value of the digits in a decimal number and partition them using place value charts. Discuss the difference between integer and decimal parts of numbers, for example understanding that 4.5 is 4 ones and 5 tenths. Students can also explore the relationship between the different place value columns, for example hundredths are 10 times the value of thousandths and one-tenth of the value of tenths. Encourage them to focus on the structure of decimal numbers, for example saying 1.35 as “one point three five” rather than “one point thirty-five”, which can lead to misconceptions such as 1.35 > 1.4
SS5_desc: In this small step, students focus on understanding how the place value of decimal numbers affects their relative positioning on a number line. They should already be confident with integer number lines and be aware of the difference between the number of marks and the number of intervals. Start with number lines where the start and end values are given, focusing on key decimals such as 0.5, 0.1, 0.2 and 0.25. As students’ confidence develops, use number lines that have a varying number of intervals and unmarked values in different positions. Students can then progress to finding the midpoints of intervals and intervals such as 0.05 and 0.001, if appropriate.
SS6_desc: In this small step, students compare and order decimal numbers. Start with decimal numbers that have the same number of decimal places, before progressing to other decimals and/or integers. It is important that students consider the values of the digits in place value order. Discuss whether all the place value columns need to be compared. For example, when comparing 4.62 and 9.18, only the ones need to be compared, but when comparing 0.294 and 0.29, all the places need to be compared. Representations such as place value charts and number lines can be used for support. Using numbers in context, such as lengths, costs and masses, may be useful.
SS7_desc: In this small step, students round numbers to different degrees of accuracy. Use number lines to help develop thinking about which two multiples a number is between and to compare a number to the midpoint of the number line. Emphasise “nearest” as meaning proximity, encouraging students to think about where a number is positioned on a number line, rather than rote-learned rules. Students need to understand the convention that numbers halfway between two multiples round to the greater multiple, for example 285 rounds to 290 to the nearest 10. Highlight that it is possible to get the same result when rounding a number to different degrees of accuracy. For example, 8004 rounds to 8000 to the nearest 10, the nearest 100 and the nearest 1000
SS8_desc: Building on the previous step, in this small step students round numbers to the nearest integer. Students need to be confident with identifying the previous and next integer of a number and finding the midpoints between those integers. Number lines can be used to help them identify which integer the number is closer to. They may need reminding that when a number is exactly halfway between two integers, the convention is to round to the greater integer. For example, 2.5 rounds to 3 to the nearest integer.
SS9_desc: In this small step, students round numbers to a specified number of decimal places. It is crucial for students to be able to identify multiples of 0.1, 0.01, and so on, to determine which two multiples a given number lies between and which multiple it is closest to. As in the previous steps, number lines are a useful representation. Remind students that numbers exactly halfway between two values round to the greater value. For example, 2.35 rounds to 2.4 when rounded to 1 decimal place. Highlight the fact that integers, including zero, can be written as numbers with one or more decimal places, for example 7 = 7.0
SS10_desc: In this extend step, students explore how to express numbers such as 10 000 as 104, laying the groundwork for understanding standard form. Calculators can be used to help introduce this concept and provide practice with large numbers, such as billions. This step builds on students’ prior knowledge of powers from their learning of squares and cube numbers at Key Stage 2 and also place value, emphasising how the position and value of the digit “1” changes with different powers. Students practise converting between powers of 10 and standard numerical form, becoming proficient in representing numbers both ways. Keep in mind, if students need more time to gain fluency with earlier steps, the content of this step will be covered again in Year 8
SS11_desc: In this extend step, students learn how to write and interpret numbers such as 8 × 109 and 4.5 × 108 Students need to understand how standard form works, rather than just counting zeros. It is important to discuss the correct notation of A × 10n, where A is a number greater than or equal to 1 and less than 10, and n is an integer. Using non-examples such as 0.8 × 104 and 5 × 100.3 is a good way to deepen students’ understanding. The intention in this block is for students to get a good understanding of the basics, rather than rushing to learning procedural rules. Standard form is studied in depth in Year 8
SS12_desc: In this extend step, students explore how to express numbers between zero and one as a power of 10 Students investigate decreasing powers of 10 and explore what happens when they reach powers of zero and below. They develop confidence with a negative power being equivalent to repeated division or multiplication by the reciprocal. For example, 10−2 = 1 ÷ 10 ÷ 10 or 10−2 = 1 × 1 102 = 1 × 1 100 Students will have been introduced to negative numbers in Key Stage 2, but it may be useful to reinforce that, for example, −2 is greater than −4
SS13_desc: In this extend step, students learn how to write and interpret numbers such as 2 × 10−3 and 2.4 × 10−3 Students can explore the patterns and connections between decimal numbers and standard form. It is important to again discuss the correct notation of A × 10n, where A is a number greater than or equal to 1 and less than 10, and n is an integer. Encourage discussions about why standard form is used and how it can be more efficient to write very small numbers in this way. This learning will be revisited in Year 8
SS1_desc_short: Students practice reading and writing integers, previously introduced up to ten million at Key Stage 2. This step follows GCSE conventions, using no separators for numbers up to 9999 and spaces from 10 000. Emphasis is on understanding integer place value, using charts and models, and applying real-world contexts.
SS2_desc_short: Students learn to calculate intervals and position integers on number lines, starting with given start/end values, then progressing to unmarked values. They apply these skills to read from common scales like rulers and weighing scales, and estimate positions of integers. Calculators may be used for support.
SS3_desc_short: Students learn to compare and order integers up to one billion using inequality symbols and terms like "greater than" and "less than". They'll identify digit values, use number lines, and distinguish between numbers and digits. Concepts are applied to real-world contexts like masses, wages, and costs.
SS4_desc_short: Students learn to identify decimal number values and partition them using place value charts. They distinguish between integer and decimal parts, understand relationships between place value columns, and correctly articulate decimal numbers to avoid misconceptions. This includes recognizing hundredths as 10 times thousandths and one-tenth of tenths.
SS5_desc_short: Students learn the impact of decimal place value on number line positioning. Building on integer number line knowledge, they start with defined start/end values, focusing on key decimals (0.5, 0.1, 0.2, 0.25). As confidence grows, they explore varying intervals, unmarked values, and midpoints, progressing to intervals like 0.05 and 0.001.
SS6_desc_short: Students learn to compare and order decimal numbers, starting with those having equal decimal places before advancing to other decimals and integers. They must consider digit values in place value order. Comparisons may require all place value columns. Tools like place value charts and number lines aid understanding.
SS7_desc_short: Students learn to round numbers to varying accuracies using number lines. They understand "nearest" as proximity and that numbers halfway between multiples round up. Examples include 285 rounding to 290 and 8004 rounding to 8000, demonstrating the same result can occur at different degrees of accuracy.
SS8_desc_short: Expanding on prior knowledge, students learn to round numbers to the nearest integer. They should identify the preceding and succeeding integers of a number, find midpoints, and use number lines for assistance. Remember, numbers halfway between two integers round up. For instance, 2.5 rounds to 3.
SS9_desc_short: Students learn to round numbers to specific decimal places, identifying multiples of 0.1, 0.01, etc. They determine which multiple a number is closest to, using number lines. Numbers halfway between two values round up. Integers, including zero, can be written with decimal places, e.g. 7 = 7.0.
SS10_desc_short: Students learn to express large numbers, like 10,000, as 10^4, using calculators for practice. This step leverages prior knowledge of powers, squares, cube numbers, and place value, focusing on how the value of "1" changes with different powers. Students practice converting between powers of 10 and standard form.
SS11_desc_short: Students learn to write and interpret numbers in standard form (A × 10n), where A is 1-10 and n is an integer. Emphasis is on understanding the concept, not just counting zeros. Non-examples (0.8 × 104, 5 × 100.3) enhance comprehension. Standard form is studied in depth in Year 8.
SS12_desc_short: Students explore expressing numbers between zero and one as powers of 10, including negative powers. They understand negative power as repeated division or multiplication by the reciprocal (e.g., 10^-2 = 1 ÷ 10 ÷ 10). Reinforcement of negative numbers from Key Stage 2 may be beneficial.
SS13_desc_short: Students learn to write and interpret numbers like 2 × 10−3 and 2.4 × 10−3, exploring patterns between decimals and standard form. They discuss the notation A × 10n, where A is 1-10 and n is an integer, and the efficiency of standard form for small numbers. Revisited in Year 8.

SS1_desc: In this small step, students use the formal method of column addition and subtraction, which should be familiar from Key Stage 2 This step can also be used to develop flexibility and efficiency in mental calculations. Encourage this through regular discussion and comparison of different approaches. The commutative laws of addition may also be discussed. Students may use concrete resources such as place value counters alongside the formal method to develop understanding and highlight key concepts, such as when an exchange can be made. Emphasise the importance of correctly lining up the digits and using placeholders.
SS2_desc: In this small step, students build on previous learning, focusing on formal methods to add and subtract decimals. Start by presenting calculations on a grid. Students can then move on to abstract calculations in columns, ensuring that attention is paid to the place value of each number. The use of zeros as placeholders should also be explored, for example when calculating 9 − 5.7, and it is worth discussing whether alternative methods could be used, such as counting on. Encourage students to check their answers using inverse operations. Provide opportunities to apply decimal addition and subtraction in context, for example using money.
SS3_desc: In this small step, students multiply and divide integers and decimals by 10, 100 and 1000 – a concept that they first met in Key Stage 2 This step can be used to check students’ conceptual understanding to ensure that they are not relying on a rote rule. Counters and place value charts are useful representations to help avoid misconceptions and develop understanding. Students should be confident with the change in value of numbers between place value columns. It may be useful to practise multiplying and dividing by 10, then 100, then 1000 in isolation, before combining them. This step provides a good opportunity to solve problems in contexts such as money.
SS4_desc: In this extend step, students further their understanding from the previous step to multiplying by 0.1 and 0.01 Emphasising the links between the fractional and decimal forms of tenths and hundredths is essential. Students may be familiar with these conversions from Key Stage 2. Highlight that multiplying by 0.1 is equivalent to dividing by 10 and that multiplying by 0.01 is equivalent to dividing by 100. Ensure that links between fractions and operations are also made, for example 3 × 0.1 = 3 × 1 10 = 3 ÷ 10 It may be useful to give students the opportunity to multiply numbers by 0.1 and 0.01 in isolation, before providing questions involving both.
SS5_desc: This small step builds on students’ conceptual understanding of multiplying integers from Key Stage 2 Students may have a variety of preferred strategies, including the formal column method and the grid method. Spend time exploring which method is likely to be most efficient and accurate; this may vary between students. Begin by modelling methods to multiply numbers by a single digit and, as students’ confidence develops, move on to multiplying by 2-digit numbers and greater. Place value counters and charts are a useful representation to highlight when and how to carry out an exchange. Encourage students to use compensation strategies for calculations such as 399 × 7 and to estimate an answer before formally calculating. The commutative laws of multiplication may also be discussed.
SS6_desc: In this small step, students revisit short division, which they should have covered in Key Stage 2 Begin by exploring how the method works, using place value counters for questions such as 96 ÷ 3, focusing on how many groups of 3 tens and 3 ones can be formed. Start with questions that create integer answers before introducing more complex questions that involve remainders. The emphasis of this step should be on expressing any remainders as decimals by using additional placeholders. The initial focus should be on dividing numbers by 2, 4, 5 and 8 to give answers with terminating decimals, before exploring divisions that produce answers with recurring decimals. Encourage students to divide numbers by 2 or more digits, for example working out 684 ÷ 12 by dividing 684 by 2, then dividing the result by 6
SS7_desc: This small step builds on prior learning covered in this block and extends to multiplying decimal numbers. Compare related integer and decimal calculations throughout, for example 2 × 3 and 2 × 0.3. Begin by multiplying decimals by integers, before multiplying decimals by decimals, encouraging students to multiply each decimal in a calculation by a multiple of 10 to make it an integer. Encourage students to estimate the answer to a calculation first, to give an idea of the relative size of the answer. Discuss compensation strategies for calculations such as 2.99 × 4. As in previous steps, it is useful to apply a context, for example money.
SS8_desc: In this small step, students divide a decimal number by an integer, building on their understanding of dividing integers. As in the previous step, look at related division facts, for example 48 ÷ 3 = 16, so 4.8 ÷ 3 = 1.6. Explore the pattern that as the number being divided becomes 10 times smaller, the answer also becomes 10 times smaller. Students should use the formal method for dividing integers, placing emphasis on grouping rather than sharing. Place value counters and charts are useful to develop students’ understanding and to highlight when exchanges should be made. As with previous steps, encourage students to estimate their answers. They can also consolidate learning from the previous step by multiplying answers by the given divisor to check answers.
SS9_desc: In this extend step, students divide integers and decimals by decimals. As with previous steps, emphasis should be placed on grouping, as sharing a given amount into non-integer groups can be a difficult concept. For example, for 6 ÷ 0.5 students should think about the number of groups of 0.5 that can be made from 6 ones. Bar models or place value counters can be useful to support this. As questions increase in difficulty, encourage students to think about equivalent calculations. For example, to work out 8 ÷ 0.2, multiplying both numbers by 10 will give an equivalent calculation involving integers: 80 ÷ 2. Comparisons could also be made between division and fractions, for example 4.5 ÷ 0.5 = 4.5 0.5 = 45 5 = 45 ÷ 5 = 9
SS10_desc: In this small step, students develop understanding of how to prioritise operations in calculations. Students should have met the order of operations at Key Stage 2, but some may be reliant on rules or acronyms such as BIDMAS. It is worth discussing the pros and cons of these, including equal priority of operations, leading to a “pyramid” of operations. Highlight that when the operations have equal priority, they should be calculated from left to right. Once students are confident, introduce questions involving roots or brackets raised to a power, for example 75 + 25 or (4 + 5)2 Most calculators use order of operations correctly, but comparing and checking answers is useful.
SS1_desc_short: Students apply column addition and subtraction, a Key Stage 2 concept, for efficient mental calculations. Discussion of various approaches and commutative laws of addition is encouraged. Use of place value counters aids understanding of key concepts like exchange. Importance of aligning digits and using placeholders is emphasized.
SS2_desc_short: Students enhance decimal addition and subtraction skills using grids and column calculations, focusing on place value and zeros as placeholders (e.g., 9 - 5.7). Alternative methods like counting on are discussed. Students verify answers with inverse operations and apply skills in real-world contexts like money.
SS3_desc_short: Students multiply and divide integers and decimals by 10, 100, and 1000, building on Key Stage 2 concepts. This step tests conceptual understanding, using counters and place value charts to clarify misconceptions. Students should grasp number value changes between place value columns and apply skills in contexts like money.
SS4_desc_short: Students deepen understanding of multiplying by 0.1 and 0.01, linking fractional and decimal forms of tenths and hundredths. Emphasize that multiplying by 0.1 equals dividing by 10, and 0.01 equals dividing by 100. Illustrate with examples, like 3 × 0.1 = 3 ÷ 10. Practice multiplying by 0.1 and 0.01 separately before combining.
SS5_desc_short: This step enhances students' understanding of multiplying integers, using strategies like the column and grid methods. It starts with single-digit multiplication, progressing to larger numbers. Tools like place value counters aid in demonstrating exchanges. Students are encouraged to estimate answers, use compensation strategies, and understand commutative laws.
SS6_desc_short: Students revisit short division from Key Stage 2, using place value counters for questions like 96 ÷ 3. They start with integer answers, then tackle remainders expressed as decimals. The focus is on dividing numbers by 2, 4, 5, and 8, then exploring recurring decimals. They're encouraged to divide by multiple digits.
SS7_desc_short: This step progresses from prior learning to multiplying decimal numbers. It involves comparing related integer and decimal calculations, starting with multiplying decimals by integers, then decimals by decimals. Students are encouraged to estimate answers, discuss compensation strategies, and apply real-world contexts like money.
SS8_desc_short: Students learn to divide a decimal by an integer, using division facts and noting that when the divided number is 10 times smaller, the answer is too. They should use formal methods, focusing on grouping, use place value counters and charts, estimate answers, and multiply to check results.
SS9_desc_short: Students learn to divide integers and decimals by decimals, focusing on grouping. They should consider the number of 0.5 groups in 6, using bar models or place value counters. As difficulty increases, students should find equivalent calculations, like multiplying both numbers in 8 ÷ 0.2 by 10.
SS10_desc_short: Students revisit the order of operations from Key Stage 2, discussing the benefits and drawbacks of rules like BIDMAS. They learn to calculate equal priority operations from left to right and tackle questions involving roots or brackets raised to a power. Checking answers with calculators is encouraged.

SS1_desc: In this small step, students construct and interpret pictograms, which they previously met in Primary. Start with examples where the key indicates that one symbol represents one or two objects. Using counters and a printed pictogram template can support students in creating a pictogram, before they move on to drawing symbols. When students are confident working with whole symbols, introduce examples with half symbols. As they become more confident, progress to pictograms with different keys and introduce examples involving fractions of symbols such as quarter and three-quarter symbols. Students could also construct pictograms from data represented in a tally chart.
SS2_desc: In this small step, students construct bar charts from tally charts and frequency tables, revisiting learning from Primary. They practise reading and interpreting data presented in bar charts. They are also introduced to vertical line charts, which are similar to bar charts but use lines instead of bars to show the frequency. The focus is on discrete data, ensuring equal gaps between bars to clearly distinguish categories. Students can explore both vertical and horizontal bar charts, understanding that while the orientation changes, the principles of accurate labelling, consistent scaling and clear data representation remain the same. Encourage them to identify trends and understand the meaning of bar heights or line positions. Ensure that students’ charts are accurately labelled and the axes are correct.
SS3_desc: In this small step, students construct, interpret and compare data using dual bar charts, revisiting learning from Primary. Draw students’ attention to the benefits of comparing two sets of related data presented side by side (either vertically or horizontally), making it easier to draw conclusions from the contrasting heights or lengths. Highlight the importance of a key to identify the data categories. Emphasise the importance of carefully planning the axis scales to ensure that they fit the data. Providing students with completed or partially completed axes can be beneficial, helping them to choose suitable scales and determine where to place gaps, making it easier to read and interpret the charts effectively.
SS4_desc: In this small step, students construct and interpret composite bar charts. Establish that in a composite bar chart, each bar represents a total, and each section of the bar a sub-category. Start with a composite bar chart with two sub-categories, for example the sales of orange juice and apple juice over a weekend. Orange juice and apple juice are the sub-categories, and the complete bar represents the total sales of drinks each day. Ensure that students understand how to compare sub-categories across the bars and can determine the frequency of sub-categories. Give students opportunities to discuss the differences between composite, dual and standard bar charts.
SS5_desc: In this small step, students read and plot coordinates in the first quadrant, building on their knowledge of coordinates from Key Stage 2 Recap key terms relating to a coordinate grid, such as the origin, the x-axis and the y-axis. Establish that a coordinate grid is used to describe and accurately show the position of points. Demonstrate how to read coordinates by first moving horizontally along the x-axis and then vertically along the y-axis. Emphasise that coordinates are written as (x, y), with the x-coordinate always coming first. Once students are confident in reading coordinates, they can plot points on the grid and explore coordinate grids with different scales on the axes.
SS6_desc: In this small step, students draw scatter graphs and interpret relationships between data sets. Explain that scatter graphs display how two sets of data relate, using pairs of coordinates plotted on a graph. Everyday examples, such as height versus shoe size, can help to make this more relatable. Explain the role of each axis in context and emphasise that every point represents a specific pair of values. Initially, provide pre-drawn axes to support students and then discuss how to select an appropriate scale for each axis, encouraging them to draw their own. Guide students to identify patterns in the data and to describe the relationships. Explore scatter graphs with positive and negative correlation, although correlation is covered in detail in the next step.
SS7_desc: In this small step, students examine scatter graphs to determine if there is a relationship between two variables. The relationship is called correlation. Data points with an upward trend show “positive correlation” and a downward trend shows “negative correlation”. If there is no clear pattern, it indicates “no correlation”. Discuss not only the direction of correlation, but also its strength, explaining that strong correlation shows points closely clustered around a trend line, whereas weak correlation shows points more spread out. Use everyday examples, for example temperature versus ice cream sales (positive correlation) or time spent practising versus mistakes made (negative correlation), to make the concept relatable.
SS8_desc: In this small step, students draw and interpret lines of best fit on a scatter graph. In Maths, a line of best fit is a straight line that best represents the overall trend between two variables and is used to predict future or missing values. A positive correlation produces a straight upward-sloping line, and a negative correlation a downward slope. If there is no clear correlation, a line of best fit cannot be drawn. Discuss how closely data points cluster around the line to indicate the strength of the relationship. Draw attention to data points that do not follow the general trend. Explain that these are called outliers and discuss possible reasons for these values. It is worth noting that outliers in a scientific context are referred to as anomalous results or an anomaly.
SS9_desc: In this small step, students draw and interpret time series graphs. A time series graph is a specific type of line graph used to display data points over time, highlighting trends and changes in a variable. Students should focus on identifying any upward or downward trends, along with periods of stability. Emphasise that while time series graphs highlight overall trends, short-term fluctuations may not always reflect the longer-term trend. Time series graphs with points joined by solid lines imply that it is possible to estimate information between data points, while points joined by dashed lines imply that data between points is not reliable. Discuss different scenarios: for example, estimating the temperature between two recorded points may make sense, but reading values between two years may not always be accurate or useful.
SS10_desc: In this small step, students identify non-linear relationships in graphs where the data points do not follow a straight-line pattern, but instead show a non-linear relationship between the variables. Students should focus on determining whether there is a relationship between the variables, even if it is not linear. A non-linear relationship may show a curve or other patterns that still indicate a correlation. For example, a U-shaped graph demonstrates that while a line of best fit cannot be drawn, there is still a clear relationship between the variables. It is important to recognise that a non-linear correlation does not mean that there is no relationship at all, as the data points may still follow a consistent pattern. Understanding non-linear relationships helps students to differentiate between different types of correlation and to interpret data more accurately.
SS1_desc_short: Students construct and interpret pictograms, starting with one symbol representing one or two objects. They use counters and pictogram templates, progressing to drawing symbols. As confidence grows, half symbols and fractions of symbols are introduced. They also create pictograms from tally chart data.
SS2_desc_short: Students learn to create bar and vertical line charts from tally charts and frequency tables, interpreting data accurately. They understand the importance of equal gaps, accurate labelling, consistent scaling, and clear data representation in both orientations. They're encouraged to identify trends and ensure correct axes.
SS3_desc_short: Students learn to construct, interpret, and compare data using dual bar charts, a concept from Primary. They understand the benefits of comparing related data sets side by side, the importance of a key for data categories, and planning axis scales. Guidance on axes completion aids in choosing scales and gap placement.
SS4_desc_short: Students learn to create and interpret composite bar charts, where each bar signifies a total and its sections represent sub-categories. Using examples like daily sales of different juices, they compare sub-categories and determine frequency. Discussions on differences between composite, dual, and standard bar charts are encouraged.
SS5_desc_short: Students learn to read and plot coordinates in the first quadrant, building on Key Stage 2 knowledge. They understand key terms like origin, x-axis, y-axis, and how to use a coordinate grid to accurately position points. They learn to read coordinates as (x, y) and explore different grid scales.
SS6_desc_short: Students learn to draw and interpret scatter graphs, understanding relationships between data sets. They use coordinates to plot data, comprehend the role of axes, and identify patterns. Examples like height versus shoe size make concepts relatable. They progress from pre-drawn to self-drawn axes, exploring positive and negative correlation.
SS7_desc_short: Students analyse scatter graphs to identify correlations between two variables. Positive correlation is an upward trend, negative correlation a downward trend, and no clear pattern indicates no correlation. Strength of correlation is discussed, with strong correlation having closely clustered points and weak correlation showing more spread points.
SS8_desc_short: Students learn to draw and interpret best fit lines on scatter graphs, understanding their use in predicting values and identifying positive, negative, or no correlation. They'll examine data point clusters for relationship strength, identify outliers, and discuss possible reasons for these anomalies.
SS9_desc_short: Students learn to draw and interpret time series graphs, identifying trends, changes, and periods of stability. They understand that solid lines allow estimation between data points, while dashed lines indicate unreliable data. Emphasis is on discerning long-term trends despite short-term fluctuations, and the potential inaccuracies in estimating between points.
SS10_desc_short: Students identify non-linear relationships in graphs, where data points don't follow a straight line but show a correlation through curves or patterns. They learn to recognise these relationships, understand that non-linearity doesn't negate correlation, and interpret data patterns accurately, differentiating various types of correlation.

Small Step 2: Number lines with fractions and decimals
Small Step 6: Convert simple fractions, decimals and percentages
Small Step 13: Fractions, decimals and percentages greater than 1 E
SS1_desc: In this small step, students recognise tenths and hundredths when represented with a diagram. Students explore diagrams such as number lines to see how tenths represent a whole divided into ten equal parts. They also look at how many hundredths are equivalent to one tenth. By introducing a hundred square as having an overall value of 1, students can determine that one small square is equivalent to 0.01 and 1 100 and hence make equivalent connections between hundredths and tenths. It is important that students can complete a visual representation as well as write in numerals and words what a given diagram represents.
SS2_desc: In this small step, students develop their understanding of using both fractional and decimal number lines and transition smoothly between the two. Start with number lines that range from 0 to 1 with ten equal increments, before progressing to number lines with different start and end values. Students also explore double number lines with both fraction and decimal values. Remind them about equivalences established from the previous step, for example 7 10 = 0.7 and model how both can be represented on a number line.
SS3_desc: In this small step, students reinforce their understanding of tenths and hundredths, while also exploring fifths and quarters. Draw attention to equivalences such as 10 100 = 1 10 = 0.1 and 2 10 = 1 5 = 0.2. Highlight the link between tenths and fifths, which can then be used to convert other values such as 3 5 to decimals. Students then progress to working with quarters, using 25 100 = 1 4 = 0.25 to convert other values such as 3 4 Representations such as hundred squares and number lines are useful to build conceptual understanding.
SS4_desc: In this small step, students continue to convert between fractions and decimals, specifically eighths and thousandths. This builds on their prior understanding of converting tenths, hundredths, fifths and quarters. Start with thousandths. Model using multipliers to scale fractions that students are already familiar with into thousandths. Linking eighths to quarters, for example 1 4 = 2 8, is a useful strategy when exploring eighths. Students may need to recap dividing decimals, for example 0.25 ÷ 2
SS5_desc: In this small step, students deepen their understanding of percentages by exploring the relationship between fractions and percentages. Emphasise that “per cent” means “per hundred”. For example, 27% represents 27 out of 100. Use a hundred square to illustrate this visually. Students practise writing the percentage of shaded and unshaded regions, recognising that 100% represents a whole. Encourage them to first express amounts as fractions, such as 27 100, before converting to percentages. Students then progress to interpreting diagrams divided into other equal parts, such as 20
SS6_desc: In this small step, students draw together their knowledge from the previous steps in this block and gain fluency in converting between simple fractions, decimals and percentages. The focus remains on familiarity with commonly seen fractions, decimals and percentages, rather than rote memorisation of a technique for any value. In particular, students should be confident in converting multiples of 10% and 25% given in any form. Start with identifying the fraction, decimal and percentage of shaded parts in diagrams, such as hundred squares and bar models, before progressing to more abstract conversions.
SS7_desc: In this small step, students develop their understanding of fractions through visual representations. They identify shaded fractions, compare fractions in shapes and accurately shade shapes to represent given fractions. Before this step, students should be familiar with basic fraction concepts, including numerators, denominators and the idea of equal parts forming a whole. This step expands their knowledge to include less common fractions and non-standard representations, reinforcing that fractions are based on equal parts, not necessarily identical shapes. Challenge students to represent a fraction in multiple ways on the same shape.
SS8_desc: In this small step, students position and read fractions on a number line between 0 and 1 Start with number lines divided into equal parts, identifying the fraction that each interval represents. This builds on prior learning and helps students to understand how moving along the number line affects the numerator. They progress to estimating the positions of fractions on a number line with only key points marked, such as halfway. Encourage students to write equivalent fractions for these, for example expressing 1 2 as 4 8 when locating 3 8. Once confident, they work with number lines that have no marked intervals, using estimation strategies such as identifying the halfway point as a reference.
SS9_desc: In this small step, students recognise, generate and compare equivalent fractions, both numerically and visually. Before this step, students need to have a strong understanding of fractions, including what the numerator and denominator represent, and be fluent with multiplication and division skills. Students create equivalent fractions by multiplying or dividing both the numerator and denominator by the same number and use visual representations such as bar models or partitioned shapes to demonstrate equivalence. They should understand that a fraction represents a number that can be written in an infinite number of ways.
SS10_desc: In this small step, students compare and order fractions. Bar models are useful to support understanding. Start with fractions that have the same denominator and compare numerators, followed by fractions with different denominators, where one is a multiple of the other, and then unrelated denominators using common multiples. To compare fractions with the same numerator, students need to understand that a greater denominator means a smaller fraction. For each comparison, they should consider whether it is more efficient to use a common numerator or common denominator. Comparing fractions to numbers such as 0, 1 or 1 2 is another useful strategy, for example 7 10 > 3 8 because 7 10 > 1 2 and 3 8 < 1 2. This helps to develop number sense and supports more efficient comparisons.
SS11_desc: In this small step, students use the fact that a fraction represents both a proportion of a whole and a division. Students use this understanding to write the division represented by a fraction and then use a calculator or short division method to convert the fraction into a decimal. This builds on the foundational understanding of how fractions and decimals are connected. Start with tenths, as this is a concept that students are already familiar with. Students progress to ordering values after completing the division represented by the fraction. Discuss how the value of the denominator impacts the final value, highlighting how the value is smaller as the denominator increases.
SS12_desc: In this small step, students build on prior knowledge to confidently convert between fractions, decimals and percentages. Encourage students to use a combination of calculator and non-calculator methods. Remind them that fractions represent division and that percentages are out of 100, which can support conversions to fractions or decimals. A recap of place value may also be useful when converting decimals to fractions, for example recognising that 0.12 represents 12 hundredths or 12 100
SS13_desc: In this extend step, students explore different representations of values greater than one, building on their understanding from the previous step. It may be useful to revisit representations of 1 whole, ensuring that students understand that 100% is equivalent to any fraction with an equal numerator and denominator, for example 3 3, and this is equivalent to 1 Students order fractions, decimals and percentages in ascending or descending order. Emphasise the importance of converting all values into an equivalent form to enable accurate comparison before ordering. The use of calculators is encouraged throughout this step to help students focus on the process of conversion rather than on arithmetic.
SS1_desc_short: Students learn to identify tenths and hundredths using diagrams like number lines and hundred squares. They understand a whole divided into ten equals tenths, and how many hundredths make a tenth. They link hundredths and tenths visually and numerically, recognising 0.01 as 1/100 of a whole.
SS2_desc_short: Students learn to use fractional and decimal number lines, transitioning between them. They start with 0-1 number lines in ten increments, then progress to varied start/end values. They explore double number lines with both fraction and decimal values, reinforcing equivalences like 7/10 = 0.7 on number lines.
SS3_desc_short: Students enhance understanding of tenths, hundredths, fifths, and quarters, learning equivalences like 10/100=1/10=0.1 and 2/10=1/5=0.2. They explore connections between tenths and fifths for conversions, like 3/5 to decimals. Progressing to quarters, they use 25/100=1/4=0.25 for conversions. Hundred squares and number lines aid conceptual understanding.
SS4_desc_short: Students progress in converting fractions to decimals, focusing on eighths and thousandths, building on prior knowledge of tenths, hundredths, fifths, and quarters. Techniques include using multipliers to scale familiar fractions into thousandths and linking eighths to quarters. Recap of dividing decimals may be necessary.
SS5_desc_short: Students enhance their understanding of percentages by exploring their connection to fractions. They learn that "per cent" signifies "per hundred", using a hundred square for visual representation. They practice writing percentages for shaded/unshaded regions, initially expressing amounts as fractions before converting to percentages. They also interpret diagrams divided into equal parts.
SS6_desc_short: Students consolidate previous knowledge to gain proficiency in converting simple fractions, decimals, and percentages. Emphasis is on familiarity with common values, especially multiples of 10% and 25%, rather than memorisation. They start with identifying fractions, decimals, and percentages in diagrams before moving to abstract conversions.
SS7_desc_short: Students enhance fraction understanding via visual representations, identifying and comparing shaded fractions, and shading shapes for given fractions. Prior knowledge of numerators, denominators, and equal parts forming a whole is required. The step includes less common fractions and non-standard representations, emphasizing fractions as equal parts, not identical shapes.
SS8_desc_short: Students learn to position and read fractions on a number line between 0 and 1, starting with equally divided lines. They estimate fraction positions on lines with key points, like halfway, and write equivalent fractions. They progress to unmarked lines, using estimation strategies like identifying halfway points.
SS9_desc_short: Students learn to identify, create, and compare equivalent fractions numerically and visually. They apply multiplication and division to both numerator and denominator, using tools like bar models or partitioned shapes for visualisation. The concept that a fraction can be represented in countless ways is emphasised.
SS10_desc_short: Students learn to compare and order fractions using bar models, starting with same denominators, then different ones. They understand a larger denominator means a smaller fraction. They consider efficiency of common numerators or denominators and compare fractions to numbers like 0, 1, or 1/2 to develop number sense.
SS11_desc_short: Students learn that fractions represent a portion of a whole and a division, which they convert into decimals using calculators or short division. They start with tenths, a familiar concept, then progress to ordering values. The impact of the denominator on the final value is discussed.
SS12_desc_short: Students enhance prior knowledge to convert between fractions, decimals, and percentages using calculator and non-calculator methods. They're reminded fractions signify division, percentages are out of 100, aiding conversions. Recapping place value assists in converting decimals to fractions, like recognising 0.12 as 12 hundredths.
SS13_desc_short: Students build on prior knowledge to explore representations of values greater than one, understanding that 100% equals any fraction with equal parts (e.g., 3/3 = 1). They order fractions, decimals, and percentages, converting all values for accurate comparison. Calculator use is encouraged to aid conversion process.

Small Step 8: Order of operations with directed numbers
Small Step 9: Use a calculator with negative and postive numbers
