Children recognise that some of the cubes in a pictorial representation cannot be seen, but that the total volume can be found by counting the number of cubes in each layer. This leads to the formula to work out the volume of a cuboid, which is covered in Year 6
SS2_desc: This small step builds on the previous step by comparing the volumes of different shapes. In Year 3, children compared the volume of liquid in different containers using simple vocabulary. In this small step, they find the volume of different shapes by counting cubes, then decide which shape has the greater volume. Begin by looking at 3-D shapes made from interlocking cubes, asking children to say which contains more cubes and so has the greater volume. Children can then move on to pictorial representations, working out the number of cubes needed to make each shape before deciding which has the greater volume. Finally, children compare cuboids. They may find it easier to make the cuboids themselves in order to work out the volume, or they may count the number of cubes in each layer, then multiply this by the height of the shape.
SS3_desc: estimate the volumes of different objects, by using cubes with a volume of 1 cm3 and building a shape similar to the 3-D object. Give children cubes and ask them to estimate the volumes of objects found in the classroom. For example, they could estimate the volume of a small book by making a similar-sized cuboid with interlocking cubes. For each object, discuss whether the actual volume is greater or less than the estimate. For example, an apple may have a smaller volume than that of a similar-sized cuboid. Children then consider the volumes of much larger objects such as rooms. They discuss why cubic centimetres would be inappropriate for larger volumes and think about the need for different units such as cubic metres.
SS4_desc: In the final small step of this block, children move on to looking at the capacity of different objects. Children should be aware of the difference between capacity and volume from earlier learning, knowing that the capacity of, for example, a jug is how much liquid the jug can hold and that volume refers to how much liquid is actually in the jug. They should also know that the term “capacity” is most commonly used when looking at amounts of liquid, and they will have met the measures litres and millilitres as far back as Year 2. They may need reminding that 1 litre is equal to 1,000 millilitres. Spend some time showing children containers of different sizes, discussing the capacity of each, then matching capacities to containers. Looking at containers that children may be more familiar with, such as a 330 millilitre can and a 2 litre bottle, will help them with estimating the capacity of unknown containers. They can then estimate the capacity of a container where a known amount of something is already inside it.
SS1_desc_short: In Year 3, students learn to measure volume using cubes, understanding it as the space an object occupies. They construct shapes, calculate their volume in cubic centimetres, and interpret pictorial representations, including hidden cubes. This foundation leads to the cuboid volume formula taught in Year 6.
SS2_desc_short: Building on prior knowledge, students compare volumes of different shapes by counting cubes. They start with 3-D shapes made from interlocking cubes, then progress to pictorial representations, calculating cubes needed for each shape. Finally, they compare cuboids, either by constructing them or multiplying cube layers by height.
SS3_desc_short: Estimate object volumes using 1 cm3 cubes to build similar shapes. Children estimate classroom object volumes, like a small book, with interlocking cubes. Discuss if actual volume is greater or less than estimates. Consider larger volumes like rooms, discussing the need for different units, such as cubic metres.
SS4_desc_short: In this step, children explore the capacity of objects, differentiating it from volume. They recall the terms "litres" and "millilitres" from Year 2, understanding 1 litre equals 1,000 millilitres. They examine various containers, match capacities, and estimate the capacity of familiar and unknown containers.

SS1_desc: Building on their experiences from earlier years, children recognise, read and write all metric measures for length, mass and capacity. This is the first time they will be introduced to tonnes as a measure for mass. Highlight the difference between capacity (the amount an object can contain) and volume (the amount actually in an object). Children consider the most appropriate unit of measure and develop their estimation skills in context. Although metric units of measurement are used throughout, children may mention imperial units of measurement. The relationship between metric and imperial units will be explored later in the block. Refer to the mass of an object, rather than its weight. The mass remains constant, whereas the weight of an object depends on the effect of gravity.
SS2_desc: In previous years, children learnt how to multiply and divide numbers by 10, 100 and 1,000. In Year 5, children learnt how to convert between metric measurements of length and mass. This small step recaps this learning and also introduces conversions between metric measurements for capacity. Children convert between units both ways, for example from metres to centimetres and centimetres to metres. When making these conversions, children may need to be reminded about decimal place value. When comparing measurements with different units, children need to convert them to the same unit. During this small step, highlight the inverse relationship between multiplication and division. It is important that children understand the role of zero as a place value holder when performing some calculations.
SS3_desc: Building on the previous step, children use and apply their conversion skills to solve measurement problems in context. The use of pictorial representations, such as bar models and number lines, to represent the problem helps children to choose the correct operation(s) to solve the problem. Children need to be secure with the four operations to find the correct numerical answers. Some of the problems involve finding a fraction of an amount (covered earlier this term) and adding and subtracting decimals, which will be revisited in the Spring Term.
SS4_desc: In Year 5, children explored the relationship between some imperial and metric units of measurement. This small step focuses on the relationship between miles and kilometres. Children need to know that one mile is a greater distance than one kilometre. They learn that 5 miles is approximately equal to 8 km. Using this fact, they solve conversions from miles to kilometres and from kilometres to miles. Children need to know that the symbol “≈” means “is approximately equal to”. To provide context, distances measured in miles in the UK could be compared to distances measured in kilometres in Europe.
SS5_desc: continue to explore imperial measures and the relationships between imperial and metric measures. Children need to know and use the following facts: • 1 inch ≈ 2.5 cm • 1 foot = 12 inches • 1 pound = 16 ounces • 1 stone = 14 pounds • 1 gallon = 8 pints They use these facts to perform related conversions, both within imperial measures and between imperial and metric measures. Attention should be drawn to the fact that the conversion between inches and cm is approximate while the others are exact.
SS1_desc_short: Building on prior knowledge, students learn to identify, read, and write all metric measures for length, mass, and capacity, including tonnes. They distinguish between capacity and volume, choose suitable units, and enhance estimation skills. References to imperial units may arise, with comparisons explored later. Emphasize mass over weight.
SS2_desc_short: Recapping multiplication, division, and metric conversions from previous years, Year 5 students learn to convert between metric measurements of capacity, such as metres to centimetres. They compare measurements in the same unit, understanding decimal place value, the inverse relationship of multiplication and division, and zero's role in calculations.
SS3_desc_short: Children apply conversion skills to solve measurement problems, using pictorial aids like bar models and number lines. Mastery of the four operations is required for accurate solutions. Problems may include finding fractions of amounts and adding/subtracting decimals, topics revisited later.
SS4_desc_short: In Year 5, students learn the relationship between miles and kilometres, understanding a mile is longer than a kilometre and 5 miles ≈ 8 km. They practice converting between these units and learn "≈" means "approximately equal to". UK and European distances provide context.
SS5_desc_short: Explore imperial and metric measures, focusing on key conversions: 1 inch ≈ 2.5 cm, 1 foot = 12 inches, 1 pound = 16 ounces, 1 stone = 14 pounds, 1 gallon = 8 pints. Understand that inch to cm conversion is approximate, others are exact. Apply these facts in related conversions.

Small Step 2: Read and plot points in four quadrants
SS1_desc: Children were first introduced to a coordinate grid in Year 4. That learning is revisited in this small step, with children looking at the first quadrant, where both the x- and y-coordinates are positive. Begin by recapping what the coordinate grid is and the names of the two axes, x and y. Then consider points on the grid. Discuss how children can find the coordinates for a given point, reading the first value on the x-axis and the second value on the y-axis. Children then move on to plotting points with given coordinates. Ensure that children understand the importance of the order of the values. Children draw shapes on a coordinate grid, suggesting possible coordinates for vertices of different shapes. Finally, they solve problems in the first quadrant without the support of grid lines, using given coordinate information to find the coordinates of other points.
SS2_desc: extend their understanding of the coordinate grid to include all four quadrants. It may be helpful to refer to these as the first (top-right), second (top-left), third (bottom-left) and fourth (bottom-right) quadrants. Show children that the x- and y-axes can both be extended through zero into negative numbers. Children plot points in each of the “new” quadrants in turn. Model that the process is the same as for the first quadrant, and emphasise that the axes behave in the same way as number lines with positive and negative numbers, which children are already familiar with. Children should recognise the pattern of positive and negative coordinates that belong in each quadrant. When children are comfortable with points in each of the quadrants, they move on to drawing shapes in the coordinate grid, using all of the quadrants. Finally, they determine which quadrant a point with given coordinates is in, without the use of a grid to support them.
SS3_desc: use their knowledge of coordinates in four quadrants to solve problems, such as working out the coordinates of vertices of polygons. Children need to be secure in reading and plotting coordinates in all four quadrants. They consider horizontal and vertical lines that go through a known coordinate, using the fact that if they know the x-coordinate of a point on a vertical line, then every point on that line will have the same x-coordinate. Similarly, every point on a horizontal line will have the same y-coordinate. Children then use this information to help find missing coordinates on shapes, both on grids with gridlines and on those without. Finally, they use the properties of shapes to solve problems on coordinate grids, for example using the fact that the opposite sides of a rectangle are equal in length.
SS4_desc: Now that children have a good understanding of coordinates in all four quadrants, in this small step they move on to translating points and shapes on a coordinate grid. They first experienced translation on a coordinate grid in Year 4, and that learning is now extended to translate in all four quadrants. Begin by recapping that translating points means to move them. Look first at translations in one direction, either left/right or up/down, before moving on to translations in both directions. Once children have recapped translating single points on a grid, they explore translating shapes, applying the same translation to each vertex of the shape. They should see that the shape looks identical after being translated, but is in a different position on the coordinate grid. Give children opportunities to describe translations as well as perform them. Encourage children to explore the effect of translations on the coordinates.
SS5_desc: Children reflected shapes in the first quadrant of coordinate grids in Year 5, both with gridlines and without, using lines parallel to the x- or y-axis. In this small step, that learning is revisited and extended to include reflections across all four quadrants. It can be useful to use mirrors to explore reflection and to see that a reflected image looks identical to the original image, but faces the opposite direction. Start with reflecting points and shapes on a coordinate grid in the x- or y-axis. Children should count how far away each vertex is from the axis and use this to work out the coordinates of each vertex in the reflected shape. They could then be stretched to reflect shapes in lines that are parallel to each axis. This should be done both with gridlines and without, giving children the opportunity to work out reflections both by counting squares and by calculation.
SS1_desc_short: In this step, children revisit the coordinate grid from Year 4, focusing on the first quadrant with positive x and y coordinates. They recap grid basics, learn to find and plot points, understand value order, draw shapes by identifying vertex coordinates, and solve problems without grid lines.
SS2_desc_short: Expand understanding of the four-quadrant coordinate grid, including negative numbers on x- and y-axes. Practice plotting points and identifying positive/negative coordinates in each quadrant. Progress to drawing shapes using all quadrants. Learn to determine a point's quadrant from given coordinates without grid assistance.
SS3_desc_short: Students apply knowledge of four-quadrant coordinates to solve problems, such as finding polygon vertices. They read and plot coordinates, understand that points on vertical and horizontal lines share x and y coordinates respectively, and use this to find missing coordinates on shapes. They also use shape properties to solve coordinate grid problems.
SS4_desc_short: Building on children's understanding of coordinates, this step involves translating points and shapes on a coordinate grid in all four quadrants. It starts with single-direction translations, then both directions. Children apply translations to shape vertices, noting identical post-translation shapes in new grid positions. They describe and perform translations, exploring their effects.
SS5_desc_short: In Year 5, students learned to reflect shapes in the first quadrant of coordinate grids, with or without gridlines. This concept is expanded to include reflections across all four quadrants, using mirrors, counting distances from the axis, and calculating coordinates. This also involves reflecting shapes along lines parallel to each axis.

Small Step 5: Solve problems with averages and range
SS1_desc: In this small step, students identify the mode from a set of data. Discuss what an average is and why averages are useful to summarise data. Questions should include data in contexts such as costs, masses or colours. It is worth establishing that the mode is unique, as it can be found for both qualitative and quantitative data. Discuss examples where there is no mode or more than one mode. Students could also find the mode presented in different ways, for example in a table. Introduce students to the term “modal value” as well as mode, as both are commonly used.
SS2_desc: In this small step, students calculate the mean from a set of data. They may be familiar with the mean, as it is the only average that is introduced in Key Stage 2 Start with discrete data, for example the mean number of students in a class. Concrete resources such as cubes are useful to demonstrate how a set of numbers can be distributed equally. It is important for students to understand the mean conceptually, to help them solve problems later. Once students are confident working out the mean of a set of data, they calculate missing values from a set of data when given the mean. Calculators may be used throughout this step, if appropriate.
SS3_desc: In this small step, students explore another average, the median. Begin by looking at lists with an odd number of values and provide students with systematic strategies to rewrite lists of values in order. Ensure that questions include a variety of contexts, for example masses, prices and lengths. Once students are confident, explore examples with an even number of values, where the median lies between two values. Encourage students to calculate the median by finding the mean of the two values in the middle. This step could also be used to revisit and build upon the mode and/or mean, covered in the previous steps.
SS4_desc: In this small step, students calculate the range of a set of values. Emphasise that the range is not an example of an average and that it instead represents the “spread” of the data. Initially, it may be useful to encourage students to write lists in ascending order, so that they can easily identify the greatest and smallest values. As confidence develops, they can work with unordered lists and can also work out a missing value from a set of data when given the range. As with previous steps, provide data in various contexts, for example money or lengths. Ensure that ranges are stated in context where appropriate, for example “The range is 5.7 cm”, rather than “The range is 5.7”.
SS5_desc: This small step reinforces the learning so far in this block, consolidating understanding of averages and range through a problem-solving approach. Discuss what is the same and what is different about averages and range, reminding students of the key features. Present students with a variety of problems in a wide range of contexts. Include missing number problems and finding averages from tables or graphs.
SS1_desc_short: Students learn to identify the mode from data sets, understanding its uniqueness in both qualitative and quantitative data. Discussion includes the concept of averages, their usefulness in data summarisation, and instances of no mode or multiple modes. The terms "mode" and "modal value" are introduced.
SS2_desc_short: Students learn to calculate the mean from data sets, starting with discrete data like class sizes. Using concrete resources like cubes helps illustrate equal distribution. Understanding the mean conceptually aids future problem-solving. Once confident, students calculate missing values given the mean. Calculators may be used if needed.
SS3_desc_short: Students learn to calculate the median from lists of values in various contexts, such as masses, prices, and lengths. They start with odd-numbered lists, then progress to even-numbered ones, finding the mean of the middle two values. This step also reinforces understanding of mode and mean.
SS4_desc_short: Students learn to calculate the range of a set of values, understanding it as data "spread", not an average. They start with ordered lists to identify extreme values, then progress to unordered lists and finding missing values given the range. Range calculation is applied in various contexts like money or lengths.
SS5_desc_short: This step strengthens understanding of averages and range via problem-solving. Discuss similarities and differences between averages and range, reminding students of key features. Provide diverse problems, including missing number issues and calculating averages from tables or graphs.

Small Step 2: Round to 2 or more significant figures
Small Step 5: Understand and use error interval notation E
SS1_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding of place value and learn to round to significant figures, with both integers and decimals. Students should understand that rounding to 1 significant figure simplifies a number to its most essential value by looking at the place value of the first non-zero digit. It can be more useful than decimal places and is a key skill in estimation, which will be covered later in the block. This should be revisited regularly whenever needed. If appropriate, students could explore this step in conjunction with the next step, where they will learn how to round to 2 or more significant figures.
SS2_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding and progress to rounding to 2 or more significant figures. Steps 1 and 2 of this block can be taught in tandem, if appropriate. Start by encouraging students to identify the value of the first significant figure in a number, before identifying 2 or more significant figures. Make students aware that they need to consider the digit after the defined significant figures to decide whether the digit is rounded up or remains the same. If necessary, remind them of the convention that a digit of 5 or greater means that the digit is rounded up. Students should also be aware that zeros are classed as significant when they serve as a placeholder within a number. For example, in 5031, zero is the second significant figure.
SS3_desc: In this small step, students estimate the answer to a calculation by rounding the numbers to 1 significant figure and performing the calculation using the rounded numbers. Estimating answers to calculations is a key skill that helps to simplify complex problems and provides approximate solutions that are often more suitable in everyday situations. By using calculations in contexts, students can apply their experiences to help understand what makes an estimate appropriate. It may not always be suitable to round to 1 significant figure, for example when estimating a square root. Encourage students to consider whether their rounding will lead to an overestimate or an underestimate. Ensure that students estimate calculations that use all four operations, powers and roots.
SS4_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding from the previous step to use estimation to solve problems. Remind students that rounding given values to 1 significant figure is a useful strategy when estimating with calculations. Present problems in a variety of contexts, for example the area of a shape or a total cost. Encourage students to discuss whether their estimates are over- or underestimates and to reflect on whether their answers are realistic and/or within a reasonable range. This approach enhances estimation skills, making estimation meaningful in various contexts. Calculations that involve subtractions or divisions may require greater attention when considering an over- or underestimate due to some cases behaving counterintuitively. For example, 39 ÷ 1.57 ≈ 40 ÷ 2 = 20, which is an underestimate despite both values being rounded up. If appropriate, students could use calculators to check their answers.
SS5_desc: In this extend step, students build on the inequality notation covered earlier in the year to formally represent the upper and lower bounds of a number that has been rounded to a given degree of accuracy. Recap inequality notation to ensure that students are comfortable using the <, >, ≤ and ≥ symbols. The use of a number line can support students to understand the range that the true value lies within. Build students’ familiarity with error intervals by starting with values rounded to powers of 10, before exploring decimals. Discuss the convention that the true value can be equal to the lower bound but not the upper bound. It may also be appropriate to discuss the fact that, for example, 3.499999999… = 3.5, so stating that an upper bound is a terminating decimal is accepted.
SS1_desc_short: Students enhance place value understanding by learning to round to significant figures, including integers and decimals. Rounding to 1 significant figure simplifies a number to its core value, aiding in estimation. This key skill should be frequently revisited and can be taught alongside rounding to 2 or more figures.
SS2_desc_short: Students enhance their understanding of rounding to multiple significant figures. They identify the first significant figure, then additional ones, considering the subsequent digit for rounding decisions. They're reminded that digits 5 or greater are rounded up, and zeros within numbers are significant placeholders, like in 5031.
SS3_desc_short: Students learn to estimate calculation answers by rounding numbers to one significant figure. This skill simplifies complex problems, offering approximate solutions for everyday situations. Students should consider if rounding leads to overestimation or underestimation, especially with square roots. They should practice estimating with all four operations, powers, and roots.
SS4_desc_short: Students expand their understanding to use estimation in problem-solving, using rounding to one significant figure. They explore various contexts like shape areas or total costs, discussing over- or underestimates and answer validity. Special attention is given to subtractions and divisions, using calculators for answer checks if needed.
SS5_desc_short: Students expand on prior inequality notation knowledge to represent the range of a rounded number's true value. They use number lines and symbols (<, >, ≤, ≥) to understand error intervals, starting with powers of 10, then decimals. The lower bound can equal the true value, but the upper bound cannot.

Small Step 1: Numbers greater than 1 in standard form
Small Step 2: Numbers between 0 and 1 in standard form
SS1_desc: In this small step, students explore positive and negative powers of 10, building the foundations for standard form. It may be useful to start by recapping multiplication and division by 10, 100 and 1000, as well as power notation, for example 102 = 10 × 10. Encourage students to make the link between increasing and decreasing powers of 10 before discussing 100 and negative powers of 10. Highlight the connection between, for example, 102 = 100 and 10 −2 = 1 100 Discuss the benefits of writing numbers in powers of 10, considering numbers such as 1 billion = 1 000 000 000 = 109, while also exploring numbers written in words.
SS2_desc: In this small step, students write numbers greater than 1 in standard form (also known as standard index form). Introduce students to the correct form, A × 10 n, where 1 ≤ A < 10 and n is an integer. Explain the difference between numbers written in standard form and numbers written as ordinary numbers. Examples of standard form numbers in context are helpful to explain the reason for writing numbers in this way. It is important for students to see non-examples of standard form, such as 0.6 × 107 or 56 × 104, to strengthen understanding. Refer to place value and discourage students from counting/adding zeros.
SS3_desc: In this small step, students now explore standard form for numbers between 0 and 1 Students learn that a number written in standard form with a negative index has a value between 0 and 1. Remind them that numbers in standard form are in the form A × 10n, where 1 ≤ A < 10 and n is an integer. Recap the effect of multiplying numbers by negative powers of 10, making links to equivalent calculations, for example 4 × 10 −2 = 4 × 0.01. Links with fractional equivalents could also be useful, for example 4 × 1 100 , discussing strategies to calculate, for example 4 ÷ 100. Encourage discussion about examples in context, to highlight why standard form is an efficient way to write small numbers.
SS4_desc: In this small step, students input standard form numbers into a calculator, exploring all four operations. Begin by asking students to input numbers in standard form into their calculators and to give the answers as ordinary numbers. Allow them time to practise using the newly introduced functions on the calculator. As their confidence develops, they can write their answers in standard form. Modelling the use of a calculator under a visualiser or an emulator can be useful to demonstrate functions that allow direct input of numbers in standard form. Note that models of calculators differ in how they return answers to standard form calculations. Encourage students to check calculator outputs carefully and ensure that they write their answers in the requested format.
SS1_desc_short: Students learn positive and negative powers of 10, laying groundwork for standard form. Recap multiplication, division by 10, 100, 1000, and power notation (e.g., 10^2 = 10 x 10). Discuss increasing, decreasing powers of 10, connections between 10^2 = 100, 10^-2 = 1/100, and writing numbers as powers of 10.
SS2_desc_short: Students learn to write numbers greater than 1 in standard form (A × 10 n, where 1 ≤ A < 10, n is an integer). They compare standard form with ordinary numbers, study examples and non-examples (e.g., 0.6 × 107, 56 × 104), and understand place value without adding zeros.
SS3_desc_short: Students explore standard form for numbers between 0 and 1, learning that a number with a negative index is less than 1. They review multiplying by negative powers of 10, equivalent calculations, and fractional equivalents. Discussion about real-life examples highlights the efficiency of standard form for small numbers.
SS4_desc_short: Students practice inputting standard form numbers into calculators, exploring all operations. They start by converting calculator results to ordinary numbers, then progress to standard form. Demonstrations using a visualiser or emulator aid understanding. Students should check calculator outputs and write answers in the correct format.
